business_rules.given_clause: An employee record exists with a job title containing administrative or clerical keywords, and the title does not match any higher-priority keyword group
business_rules.when_clause: The EEO-1 category mapping is performed for an employee whose job title (case-insensitive) contains any of: 'admin', 'assistant', 'coordinator', 'clerk'
business_rules.then_clause: The employee is classified under the EEOC EEO-1 category 'Administrative Support'
business_rules.business_impact: Administrative and clerical staff would be misclassified as 'Professionals' by default, inflating the Professionals category and zeroing out Administrative Support in EEO-1 reports, which could mask demographic disparities in support roles and trigger EEOC scrutiny or audit findings.

business_rules.title: Default Professionals Classification for Unmatched Titles
business_rules.domain: HR Compliance / Equal Employment Opportunity Reporting
business_rules.given_clause: An employee record exists whose job title does not contain any keyword matching the defined executive, managerial, professional, technician, sales, or administrative categories
business_rules.when_clause: The EEO-1 category mapping is performed and no keyword rule has matched
business_rules.then_clause: The employee is classified under the EEOC EEO-1 category 'Professionals' as the default fallback
business_rules.business_impact: Without a default, unrecognised job titles would cause a runtime error or return None, breaking EEO-1 report generation entirely. With a wrong default category, employees with non-standard or novel job titles would be silently misclassified, potentially skewing multiple demographic cells in the EEO-1 filing and creating compliance risk if the misclassification is material.

business_rules.title: Case-Insensitive Job Title Matching
business_rules.domain: HR Compliance / Equal Employment Opportunity Reporting
business_rules.given_clause: An employee record exists with a job title stored in any combination of upper and lower case characters
business_rules.when_clause: The EEO-1 category mapping normalises the job title to lowercase before performing keyword matching
business_rules.then_clause: The keyword matching is performed uniformly regardless of the original capitalisation of the job title, ensuring consistent category assignment
business_rules.business_impact: Without case normalisation, employees with titles like 'VP of Engineering' or 'VP OF ENGINEERING' would fail to match the 'vp' keyword and fall through to the default 'Professionals' category, causing systematic misclassification of executives and other roles whose titles are stored in mixed or upper case.

business_rules.title: Null/Missing Job Title Handling
business_rules.domain: HR Compliance / Equal Employment Opportunity Reporting
business_rules.given_clause: An employee record exists where the 'job_title' field is null, missing, or an empty string
business_rules.when_clause: The EEO-1 category mapping attempts to retrieve and normalise the job title
business_rules.then_clause: The job title is treated as an empty string, no keyword matches occur, and the employee is assigned the default 'Professionals' category
business_rules.business_impact: Without this guard, a null job title would cause an AttributeError on .lower(), crashing EEO-1 report generation. With the guard but a different default, employees with missing titles would be silently placed in an arbitrary category, potentially inflating that category's count in the EEOC filing.

business_rules.title: Priority Ordering of EEO-1 Category Rules
business_rules.domain: HR Compliance / Equal Employment Opportunity Reporting
business_rules.given_clause: An employee record exists whose job title contains keywords that could match multiple EEO-1 category rules (e.g., 'Sales Director' matches both executive and sales keywords)
business_rules.when_clause: The EEO-1 category mapping evaluates rules sequentially from highest to lowest organisational level
business_rules.then_clause: The first matching rule in the priority sequence (Executive > First/Mid-Level Officials > Professionals > Technicians > Sales Workers > Administrative Support) determines the category, and lower-priority rules are not evaluated
business_rules.business_impact: Changing the evaluation order would cause employees with ambiguous titles (e.g., 'Sales Director', 'Lead Analyst', 'Admin Manager') to be assigned to a different EEO-1 category, altering the demographic distribution across categories in the EEOC filing and potentially misrepresenting the organisational hierarchy in compliance reports.

business_rules.title: Non-Exempt Employee Overtime Compensation Requirement
business_rules.given_clause: An active, non-exempt employee has worked more than 40 hours in a pay period and a valid position record exists for that employee
business_rules.when_clause: The overtime compliance check is run for the pay period
business_rules.then_clause: A violation is recorded if the employee was not paid at the 1.5x overtime rate for hours exceeding 40, identifying the employee, total hours worked, and overtime hours owed
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would allow non-exempt employees to work overtime without receiving legally mandated 1.5x pay, exposing the organization to FLSA wage-and-hour lawsuits, back-pay liability, civil penalties, and potential class-action suits from affected employees.

business_rules.title: FLSA Overtime Threshold at 40 Hours Per Week
business_rules.given_clause: An active, non-exempt employee has a recorded hours-worked value for the pay period
business_rules.when_clause: The overtime compliance check evaluates whether overtime applies
business_rules.then_clause: Overtime is only triggered when weekly hours exceed exactly 40; hours at or below 40 do not constitute an overtime violation
business_rules.business_impact: Changing this threshold (e.g., lowering it) would generate false violations and unnecessary payroll costs; raising it would cause genuine overtime violations to go undetected, creating legal liability under FLSA.

business_rules.title: Exempt Employee Minimum Salary Threshold for Valid Classification
business_rules.given_clause: An active employee is classified as exempt in their position record
business_rules.when_clause: The overtime compliance check evaluates the employee's base salary
business_rules.then_clause: A misclassification violation is recorded if the employee's base salary is below the FLSA minimum threshold of $35,568 per year, indicating the exempt classification may be legally invalid
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would allow employees earning below the FLSA salary-basis threshold to be misclassified as exempt, denying them overtime protections they are legally entitled to. This creates significant DOL audit risk, back-pay liability, and potential willful-violation penalties under FLSA.

business_rules.title: Compliance Audit Scope Limited to Active Employees Only
business_rules.given_clause: A pay period date range is provided for the compliance check
business_rules.when_clause: The system retrieves the employee population to audit
business_rules.then_clause: Only active (non-terminated) employees are included in the compliance check; terminated employees are excluded from violation assessment
business_rules.business_impact: If terminated employees were included, the report would generate spurious violations for individuals no longer employed, inflating violation counts, wasting remediation effort, and potentially triggering incorrect payroll corrections for former employees.

business_rules.title: Skip Compliance Check for Employees Without a Matched Position Record
business_rules.given_clause: An active employee exists in the system
business_rules.when_clause: No position record can be found matching the employee's job title and department
business_rules.then_clause: The employee is silently skipped and not evaluated for overtime or misclassification violations
business_rules.business_impact: Silently skipping employees with missing position records means those employees receive no compliance coverage. If a non-exempt employee lacks a position record, unpaid overtime violations will go undetected. This could be a data-quality gap that masks real violations and should ideally generate a data-integrity warning rather than a silent skip.

business_rules.title: Compliance Report Marks Period as Fully Compliant Only When Zero Violations Exist
business_rules.given_clause: The compliance check has evaluated all active employees for the pay period
business_rules.when_clause: The violations list is assembled and the report is returned
business_rules.then_clause: The 'compliant' flag in the report is set to true only if no violations were found; any single violation causes the period to be marked non-compliant
business_rules.business_impact: If the compliant flag used a threshold (e.g., compliant if fewer than N violations), downstream audit systems or dashboards might incorrectly report a non-compliant period as compliant, masking legal exposure. The all-or-nothing standard ensures any violation triggers review.

business_rules.title: Employee Must Be In PROBATION Status To Complete Probation
business_rules.given_clause: An employee record exists in the system with a status other than PROBATION (e.g., ACTIVE, TERMINATED, ON_LEAVE)
business_rules.when_clause: A request is made to complete probation for that employee
business_rules.then_clause: The system raises a ValueError and rejects the transition, preserving the employee's current status unchanged
business_rules.business_impact: Without this guard, employees who are already ACTIVE, TERMINATED, or in any other lifecycle state could be incorrectly transitioned to ACTIVE, corrupting employment status history, triggering erroneous payroll or benefits changes, and undermining audit trails. It could also allow re-activation of terminated employees through an unintended pathway.

business_rules.title: Minimum 90 Calendar Days Must Elapse Since Hire Before Probation Completion
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is currently in PROBATION status but fewer than 90 calendar days have elapsed since their hire date
business_rules.when_clause: A request is made to complete probation for that employee
business_rules.then_clause: The system raises a ValueError indicating the number of days elapsed versus the required 90, and the employee remains in PROBATION status
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would allow employees to be confirmed as permanent staff before completing the mandatory 90-day evaluation window. This could expose the organisation to legal and contractual risk (e.g., premature entitlement to full employment protections, benefits, or notice periods), undermine performance management processes, and violate HR policy or labour regulations that mandate a minimum probationary period.

business_rules.title: Successful Probation Completion Transitions Employee Status To ACTIVE
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is in PROBATION status and at least 90 calendar days have elapsed since their hire date
business_rules.when_clause: A request is made to complete probation for that employee and all validations pass
business_rules.then_clause: The employee's status is updated to ACTIVE, the updated_at timestamp is refreshed to the current UTC time, the record is persisted to the database, and the updated employee record is returned
business_rules.business_impact: If this transition were removed or inverted, employees who have legitimately served their probation period would never be promoted to ACTIVE status, blocking downstream processes that depend on ACTIVE status (e.g., payroll tier changes, benefits enrolment, access provisioning, performance review cycles). Employees could be indefinitely stuck in PROBATION, creating legal exposure and operational disruption.

business_rules.title: Exclude Terminated Employees from Headcount
business_rules.given_clause: A headcount report is being generated as of a specific date, and the employee dataset includes employees with various statuses including TERMINATED
business_rules.when_clause: The system iterates over employees retrieved as of the report date
business_rules.then_clause: Employees with a status of TERMINATED must be excluded from all headcount aggregations (total, by department, by employment type, and by status)
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, terminated employees would be counted in headcount figures, inflating workforce numbers. This would misrepresent active workforce size, distort department-level staffing metrics, corrupt employment-type breakdowns, and potentially affect budget planning, compliance reporting, and workforce capacity decisions based on inaccurate data.

business_rules.title: Include Active Employees in Headcount
business_rules.given_clause: A headcount report is being generated and an employee has a status of ACTIVE
business_rules.when_clause: The system processes each non-terminated employee
business_rules.then_clause: Active employees must be counted in the total headcount and aggregated by department, employment type, and status
business_rules.business_impact: If active employees were excluded, the headcount report would fail to represent the core working population, rendering the report useless for workforce planning, budgeting, and compliance purposes.

business_rules.title: Include On-Leave Employees in Headcount
business_rules.given_clause: A headcount report is being generated and an employee has a status of ON_LEAVE
business_rules.when_clause: The system processes each non-terminated employee
business_rules.then_clause: On-leave employees must be counted in the total headcount and aggregated by department, employment type, and status
business_rules.business_impact: If on-leave employees were excluded, the organisation would undercount its total headcount, misrepresent departmental staffing levels, and potentially violate labour law reporting requirements that mandate counting employees on protected leave (e.g., maternity, medical) as part of the workforce.

business_rules.title: Include Probationary Employees in Headcount
business_rules.given_clause: A headcount report is being generated and an employee has a status of PROBATIONARY
business_rules.when_clause: The system processes each non-terminated employee
business_rules.then_clause: Probationary employees must be counted in the total headcount and aggregated by department, employment type, and status
business_rules.business_impact: If probationary employees were excluded, the organisation would undercount its actual workforce, potentially misrepresent hiring activity, and fail to account for employees who have legal employment rights and cost implications even during probation.

business_rules.title: Headcount Report Defaults to Current Date When No Date Provided
business_rules.given_clause: A request is made to generate a headcount report without specifying an as-of date
business_rules.when_clause: The headcount report function is invoked with no date argument or a null date argument
business_rules.then_clause: The report must default to today's date as the reference point for employee data retrieval and the report output
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, callers would be forced to always supply a date, increasing integration complexity. More critically, omitting this default could cause runtime errors or require callers to independently determine 'today', risking inconsistency in what date is used for point-in-time reporting.

business_rules.title: Headcount Report Supports Point-in-Time Historical Reporting
business_rules.given_clause: A specific historical or future date is provided as the as-of date
business_rules.when_clause: The headcount report function is invoked with an explicit date
business_rules.then_clause: The report must reflect the employee population as it existed on that specific date, not the current date
business_rules.business_impact: If removed and the system always used today's date, the organisation would lose the ability to produce historical headcount snapshots required for audits, regulatory filings, trend analysis, and period-end HR reporting (e.g., end-of-quarter or end-of-year workforce counts).

business_rules.title: Employees with Unknown Department Are Attributed to 'Unknown' Department
business_rules.given_clause: An employee record references a department ID that does not resolve to a valid department in the data store
business_rules.when_clause: The system looks up the department name during headcount aggregation
business_rules.then_clause: The employee must still be counted and attributed to a department named 'Unknown' rather than being dropped or causing an error
business_rules.business_impact: If removed and unresolvable departments caused employees to be dropped, the total headcount would be silently understated. If it caused an error, the entire report would fail. The 'Unknown' bucket ensures data completeness and signals a data quality issue that can be investigated separately.

business_rules.title: Headcount Is Aggregated Simultaneously by Department, Employment Type, and Status
business_rules.given_clause: A non-terminated employee is being processed for the headcount report
business_rules.when_clause: The system counts the employee toward the report totals
business_rules.then_clause: The employee must be counted in all three dimensions — by department (cross-referenced with status), by employment type, and by status — as well as in the overall total headcount
business_rules.business_impact: If any dimension were removed, stakeholders relying on that breakdown for workforce planning, cost allocation, or compliance reporting would lose visibility. For example, removing employment-type aggregation would prevent analysis of full-time vs. part-time vs. contractor ratios, which is critical for labour cost management and regulatory compliance.

business_rules.title: Department Must Exist Before Hiring
business_rules.given_clause: A hiring request is submitted with a department ID
business_rules.when_clause: The specified department ID does not correspond to any existing department in the system
business_rules.then_clause: The hire is rejected with a ValueError indicating the department was not found, and no employee record is created
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, employees could be hired into non-existent departments, causing orphaned records, broken org-chart hierarchies, broken payroll cost-centre assignments, and downstream reporting failures.

business_rules.title: Department Headcount Limit Must Not Be Exceeded
business_rules.given_clause: A department has a positive headcount limit configured and has already reached that limit with active employees
business_rules.when_clause: A new employee hire is attempted for that department
business_rules.then_clause: The hire is rejected with a ValueError stating the department has reached its headcount limit, and no employee record is created
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would allow unlimited hiring beyond approved headcount budgets, violating workforce planning controls, breaching budget authorisations, and potentially triggering compliance or audit failures related to approved headcount.

business_rules.title: Salary Must Fall Within Position Grade Band
business_rules.given_clause: A recognised position exists for the given job title and department with a defined minimum and maximum salary grade band
business_rules.when_clause: The proposed base salary for a new hire is either below the minimum or above the maximum of that grade band
business_rules.then_clause: The hire is rejected with a ValueError specifying the offered salary and the permissible grade band range, and no employee record is created
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, employees could be hired at arbitrary salaries — either below legal or contractual minimums (creating legal liability) or above approved maximums (causing budget overruns, pay-equity violations, and compensation band integrity failures).

business_rules.title: Full-Time and Part-Time Hires Start in Probation Status
business_rules.given_clause: A new employee is being hired with an employment type of FULL_TIME or PART_TIME
business_rules.when_clause: The employee record is created
business_rules.then_clause: The employee's initial employment status is set to PROBATION, reflecting a 90-day probationary period before full employment rights apply
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would grant full-time and part-time employees immediate ACTIVE status, bypassing the probationary period. This could prematurely grant full employment entitlements (e.g., notice periods, redundancy rights, performance management protections) before the organisation has assessed suitability, creating legal and HR policy exposure.

business_rules.title: Contract and Intern Hires Start as Immediately Active
business_rules.given_clause: A new employee is being hired with an employment type other than FULL_TIME or PART_TIME (e.g., CONTRACT or INTERN)
business_rules.when_clause: The employee record is created
business_rules.then_clause: The employee's initial employment status is set to ACTIVE immediately, with no probationary period applied
business_rules.business_impact: If this rule were removed and contractors/interns were placed in PROBATION, it could incorrectly restrict their system access, delay onboarding task completion, and misrepresent their employment terms — contractors and interns typically operate under fixed-term agreements that do not include a probationary framework.

business_rules.title: Hire Date Defaults to Today If Not Provided
business_rules.given_clause: A hiring request is submitted without an explicit hire date
business_rules.when_clause: The employee record is created
business_rules.then_clause: The hire date is automatically set to the current calendar date, ensuring no employee record exists without a valid hire date
business_rules.business_impact: Without this default, omitting a hire date could result in null hire dates, breaking tenure calculations, benefits eligibility windows, probation end-date computations, and payroll start-date logic.

business_rules.title: Onboarding Workflow Must Be Triggered for Every New Hire
business_rules.given_clause: A new employee record has been successfully validated and persisted
business_rules.when_clause: The hire process completes
business_rules.then_clause: The onboarding workflow is automatically triggered, initiating tasks such as IT setup, badge provisioning, and orientation scheduling without requiring manual intervention
business_rules.business_impact: If onboarding is not automatically triggered, new hires may not receive system access, security badges, or orientation scheduling, leading to delayed productivity, poor employee experience, security gaps, and compliance failures around mandatory onboarding activities.

business_rules.title: Only Active Employees Can Be Transferred
business_rules.given_clause: An employee exists in the HR system with a status other than ACTIVE (e.g., TERMINATED, SUSPENDED, ON_PROBATION)
business_rules.when_clause: A transfer request is submitted for that employee to a new department
business_rules.then_clause: The transfer is rejected with an error stating 'Only active employees can be transferred'
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would allow terminated, suspended, or otherwise inactive employees to be transferred between departments, creating ghost headcount in departments, corrupting workforce data, and potentially triggering payroll or benefits processing for ineligible individuals.

business_rules.title: Transfer Prohibited During Active Leave
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is currently on an active leave of absence (e.g., maternity leave, sick leave, unpaid leave)
business_rules.when_clause: A transfer request is submitted for that employee
business_rules.then_clause: The transfer is rejected with an error stating 'Cannot transfer an employee who is currently on leave'
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would allow employees on leave to be transferred mid-leave, potentially disrupting leave entitlements, return-to-work arrangements, leave approvals tied to the original department, and compliance with labour law obligations that may be department- or jurisdiction-specific.

business_rules.title: Target Department Must Exist
business_rules.given_clause: A transfer request references a target department ID
business_rules.when_clause: The system attempts to look up the target department and it is not found in the data store
business_rules.then_clause: The transfer is rejected with an error stating the department was not found
business_rules.business_impact: Without this check, employees could be assigned to non-existent departments, resulting in orphaned records, broken organisational hierarchy reporting, and failures in downstream systems that rely on valid department references (e.g., payroll cost centres, access control).

business_rules.title: Target Department Must Not Exceed Headcount Capacity
business_rules.given_clause: A target department has a defined headcount limit greater than zero, and the current number of active employees in that department has reached or exceeded that limit
business_rules.when_clause: A transfer request is submitted to move an employee into that department
business_rules.then_clause: The transfer is rejected with an error stating the target department is at headcount capacity
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would allow departments to exceed their approved headcount budgets, violating workforce planning constraints, breaching budget approvals, and potentially triggering compliance issues with staffing agreements or regulatory headcount caps. It would undermine the integrity of organisational design controls.

business_rules.title: New Salary Must Fall Within Grade Band of New Position
business_rules.given_clause: A transfer includes both a new job title and a new salary, and the new job title maps to a position with a defined salary grade band (minimum and maximum) in the target department
business_rules.when_clause: The proposed new salary is either below the minimum or above the maximum of the grade band for that position
business_rules.then_clause: The transfer is rejected with an error stating the salary is outside the grade band for the specified job title
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would allow employees to be transferred with salaries that violate the organisation's compensation framework, leading to pay equity issues, audit failures, breaches of remuneration policy, and potential legal exposure under equal pay legislation. It would also undermine the integrity of job grading and compensation band structures.

business_rules.title: Transfer History Must Be Recorded
business_rules.given_clause: All business validations have passed and an employee transfer is approved
business_rules.when_clause: The employee record is updated with the new department details
business_rules.then_clause: A transfer history record is persisted capturing the employee ID, the originating department, the destination department, and the effective date of the transfer
business_rules.business_impact: Without recording transfer history, the organisation loses an auditable trail of employee movements, making it impossible to reconstruct workforce history for compliance audits, legal disputes, seniority calculations, or historical reporting. It would also break any downstream processes that rely on transfer events (e.g., triggering onboarding workflows, notifying payroll of cost centre changes).

business_rules.title: Employee Must Exist
business_rules.given_clause: An enrollment request is submitted with an employee ID
business_rules.when_clause: The system attempts to retrieve the employee record
business_rules.then_clause: The enrollment is rejected with an error if no employee record is found for the given ID
business_rules.business_impact: Without this check, enrollments could be created for non-existent employees, resulting in orphaned benefit records, incorrect payroll deductions, and potential financial liability for the employer with no corresponding employee to bill.

business_rules.title: Employee Must Be Eligible for Benefits
business_rules.given_clause: A valid employee record exists
business_rules.when_clause: An enrollment is attempted for that employee
business_rules.then_clause: The enrollment is rejected if the employee is not eligible (i.e., not full-time, still in probation, or terminated)
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would allow part-time workers, contractors, or employees still in their probationary period to enroll in benefit plans, exposing the company to unbudgeted benefit costs and potential violations of plan eligibility rules that could jeopardize the plan's tax-qualified status.

business_rules.title: Benefit Plan Must Exist
business_rules.given_clause: An enrollment request references a specific plan ID
business_rules.when_clause: The system attempts to retrieve the benefit plan record
business_rules.then_clause: The enrollment is rejected with an error if no plan record is found for the given plan ID
business_rules.business_impact: Without this check, enrollments could reference non-existent plans, creating invalid records that cannot be processed for coverage or payroll deductions, and potentially leaving employees believing they have coverage they do not actually have.

business_rules.title: Benefit Plan Must Be Active
business_rules.given_clause: A valid benefit plan record is retrieved
business_rules.when_clause: An enrollment is attempted for that plan
business_rules.then_clause: The enrollment is rejected if the plan is marked as inactive
business_rules.business_impact: Allowing enrollment in inactive plans could result in employees being enrolled in discontinued, expired, or sunset plans that no longer have active carrier contracts, leaving employees without actual coverage despite believing they are enrolled.

business_rules.title: Enrollment Must Occur Within a Valid Enrollment Window
business_rules.given_clause: An eligible employee attempts to enroll in an active benefit plan
business_rules.when_clause: The enrollment request is processed
business_rules.then_clause: The enrollment is rejected if the current date does not fall within a new hire window (30 days of hire), open enrollment period (November), or a qualifying life event window
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would allow employees to enroll in or change benefits at any time throughout the year, violating IRS regulations governing pre-tax benefit elections, potentially invalidating the tax-qualified status of plans such as FSA, HSA, and 401k, and creating administrative chaos for HR and payroll teams.

business_rules.title: No Duplicate Enrollment in the Same Benefit Type
business_rules.given_clause: An eligible employee is within an enrollment window and attempts to enroll in a benefit plan
business_rules.when_clause: The system checks for existing active enrollments of the same benefit type
business_rules.then_clause: The enrollment is rejected if the employee already has an active enrollment in a plan of the same benefit type
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, employees could enroll in multiple medical, dental, or retirement plans simultaneously, resulting in duplicate payroll deductions, over-contribution to tax-advantaged accounts (violating IRS limits), and complex reconciliation issues with insurance carriers.

business_rules.title: Dependents Not Allowed on Plans That Prohibit Them
business_rules.given_clause: An enrollment request includes one or more dependents
business_rules.when_clause: The plan being enrolled in does not allow dependents
business_rules.then_clause: The enrollment is rejected with an error indicating the plan does not support dependents
business_rules.business_impact: Allowing dependents on plans that prohibit them would violate the plan's carrier contract terms, potentially invalidating coverage for the employee and their dependents, and exposing the employer to liability for claims that the carrier would rightfully deny.

business_rules.title: Dependent Count Must Not Exceed Plan Maximum
business_rules.given_clause: An enrollment request includes dependents on a plan that allows them
business_rules.when_clause: The number of dependents specified exceeds the plan's maximum allowed dependent count
business_rules.then_clause: The enrollment is rejected with an error citing the plan's maximum dependent limit
business_rules.business_impact: Exceeding the plan's dependent maximum would violate carrier contract terms, result in incorrect premium calculations, and potentially cause claims to be denied for dependents beyond the allowed limit, creating legal and financial exposure for the employer.

business_rules.title: 401k Annual Contribution Must Pass IRS Validation
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is enrolling in a 401k retirement plan and provides an annual contribution amount
business_rules.when_clause: The contribution amount is validated
business_rules.then_clause: The enrollment is rejected if the annual contribution fails IRS limit validation (delegated to _validate_401k_contribution), and the monthly employee and employer contributions are calculated from the annual amount and employer match formula
business_rules.business_impact: Without this validation, employees could over-contribute to their 401k beyond IRS annual limits (e.g., $23,000 for 2024), triggering mandatory corrective distributions, tax penalties for the employee, and potential plan disqualification penalties for the employer.

business_rules.title: HSA Annual Contribution Must Not Exceed IRS Limit Based on Coverage Level
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is enrolling in an HSA plan and provides an annual contribution amount
business_rules.when_clause: The contribution amount is validated against the applicable IRS limit
business_rules.then_clause: The enrollment is rejected if the contribution exceeds the family HSA limit (for non-employee-only coverage) or the individual HSA limit (for employee-only coverage), and the monthly contribution is set to the annual amount divided by 12 if valid
business_rules.business_impact: Exceeding IRS HSA contribution limits results in the excess amount being subject to income tax plus a 6% excise tax penalty for the employee. The employer could also face compliance issues and reputational damage for facilitating non-compliant HSA contributions.

business_rules.title: FSA Annual Contribution Must Not Exceed IRS Limit
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is enrolling in an FSA plan and provides an annual contribution amount
business_rules.when_clause: The contribution amount is validated against the IRS FSA annual limit
business_rules.then_clause: The enrollment is rejected if the contribution exceeds the FSA IRS limit, and the monthly contribution is set to the annual amount divided by 12 if valid
business_rules.business_impact: Exceeding the IRS FSA contribution limit (e.g., $3,050 for 2024) would cause the plan to lose its tax-advantaged status for the excess amount, resulting in tax liability for the employee and potential plan disqualification, which would eliminate pre-tax benefits for all participants.

business_rules.title: HSA Coverage Level Determines Applicable IRS Contribution Limit
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is enrolling in an HSA plan with a specified coverage level
business_rules.when_clause: The HSA annual contribution limit is determined
business_rules.then_clause: The family HSA limit applies when coverage level is anything other than 'employee_only', and the individual HSA limit applies when coverage level is 'employee_only'
business_rules.business_impact: Applying the wrong IRS limit tier could allow employees with individual coverage to contribute at the higher family rate (over-contribution penalty) or restrict employees with family coverage to the lower individual limit (under-utilization of a legal tax benefit), both causing financial harm to employees.

business_rules.title: Enrollment Effective Date Is Calculated Based on Employee Context
business_rules.given_clause: All validations have passed and an enrollment record is being created
business_rules.when_clause: The effective date of the enrollment is determined
business_rules.then_clause: The effective date is set by the _calculate_effective_date business logic based on the employee's context (e.g., new hire date, life event date, or open enrollment start date)
business_rules.business_impact: An incorrect effective date could result in coverage gaps (employee has no coverage when they believe they do) or retroactive coverage liability (employer pays premiums for periods before the intended start), both creating financial and legal exposure.

business_rules.title: Successful Enrollment Creates an Active Enrollment Record
business_rules.given_clause: All business rule validations have passed for an enrollment request
business_rules.when_clause: The enrollment record is persisted
business_rules.then_clause: A new BenefitEnrollment record is created with ACTIVE status, a unique enrollment ID, the specified coverage level, dependent count, calculated employee and employer contributions, and the computed effective date, and is saved to the database
business_rules.business_impact: If the enrollment record is not persisted or is created with incorrect status, the employee would have no record of coverage, payroll deductions would not be set up, and the carrier would not be notified of the enrollment, leaving the employee without actual benefits coverage.

business_rules.title: Medical Coverage Waiver Requires Proof of Alternative Coverage
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is attempting to waive their medical benefit coverage during an enrollment window
business_rules.when_clause: The benefit type being waived is MEDICAL and the employee does not have documented alternative coverage on file
business_rules.then_clause: The waiver request is rejected with an error indicating that proof of alternative coverage is required, and no waiver is recorded
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would allow employees to waive medical coverage without any alternative coverage in place, potentially leaving employees uninsured. This could expose the organization to legal liability under healthcare mandates (e.g., ACA in the US), increase employee health risk, and create compliance violations with regulatory bodies. It could also result in financial penalties for the employer if minimum coverage requirements are not met.

business_rules.title: Waiver of Active Enrollment is Recorded for Compliance Tracking
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has an active enrollment record for the specified benefit type
business_rules.when_clause: The employee successfully waives the benefit (all preconditions such as alternative coverage proof are satisfied)
business_rules.then_clause: The existing active enrollment record is updated to a WAIVED status and persisted to the database, creating an auditable compliance record of the waiver decision
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would mean waivers are not formally recorded in the benefits system. This would break compliance audit trails, making it impossible to demonstrate regulatory adherence during audits. It could also cause downstream issues such as incorrect benefits billing, failure to notify insurers of coverage termination, and inability to resolve employee disputes about their coverage elections. The organization could face penalties for non-compliance with benefits reporting requirements (e.g., ERISA, ACA reporting).

business_rules.title: Waiver Action is Logged for Operational Compliance Tracking
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has initiated a benefit waiver request
business_rules.when_clause: The waiver process completes (regardless of whether an active enrollment existed)
business_rules.then_clause: A log entry is created recording the employee ID and the benefit type that was waived, providing an operational audit trail
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this logging would reduce the operational audit trail available for compliance investigations, HR inquiries, and debugging. While the database record (WAIVED status) provides the primary compliance record, the log provides a timestamped, independent secondary record. Its absence could hinder forensic investigations and make it harder to reconstruct the sequence of events during benefits disputes or regulatory audits.

business_rules.title: Benefits Continue Through End of Termination Month
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has active benefit enrollments and a termination date is provided
business_rules.when_clause: The employee separation/termination process is triggered
business_rules.then_clause: All active benefit enrollments must have their end date set to the last calendar day of the month in which the termination date falls, not the termination date itself
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or inverted, employees could lose benefits on their exact termination date rather than month-end, potentially leaving them without coverage mid-month for medical events. This could expose the company to legal liability, violate employment contracts or regulatory requirements, and cause significant employee harm and dissatisfaction.

business_rules.title: All Active Enrollments Are Terminated Upon Employee Separation
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has one or more active benefit enrollments on record
business_rules.when_clause: The employee termination process is executed
business_rules.then_clause: Every active enrollment for that employee must have its status updated to TERMINATED and its termination date recorded
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, terminated employees would retain active benefit enrollments indefinitely, resulting in continued premium payments for ineligible individuals, potential insurance fraud exposure, audit failures, and significant financial losses to the organization.

business_rules.title: COBRA Eligibility Applies Only to Medical, Dental, and Vision Plans
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is being terminated and has active benefit enrollments
business_rules.when_clause: Each enrollment is evaluated during the termination process
business_rules.then_clause: Only enrollments associated with benefit plans of type MEDICAL, DENTAL, or VISION are flagged as COBRA-eligible; other benefit types (e.g., 401k, life insurance, FSA) are excluded from COBRA notification
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or expanded incorrectly, the company could send COBRA notices for ineligible plan types, creating legal confusion and administrative burden, or fail to send required COBRA notices for eligible plans, resulting in regulatory violations under ERISA and potential fines of up to $110 per day per qualified beneficiary for non-compliance.

business_rules.title: COBRA Initiation Is Triggered When At Least One Eligible Enrollment Exists
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is being terminated and has at least one active enrollment in a COBRA-eligible plan (medical, dental, or vision)
business_rules.when_clause: The termination process completes evaluation of all active enrollments
business_rules.then_clause: The COBRA continuation coverage initiation process must be triggered, passing the employee ID, the list of eligible enrollments, and the benefit end date
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, terminated employees with qualifying coverage would not receive COBRA election notices, violating the federal COBRA mandate under ERISA. This exposes the employer to significant legal penalties, potential lawsuits from employees who incur medical expenses without coverage, and regulatory enforcement actions by the Department of Labor.

business_rules.title: 401k Is Handled via Employee Election (Rollover or Distribution), Not COBRA
business_rules.given_clause: An employee with an active 401k enrollment is being terminated
business_rules.when_clause: The termination process evaluates benefit plan types for COBRA eligibility
business_rules.then_clause: The 401k plan is explicitly excluded from COBRA processing and must instead be handled through a separate rollover or distribution process per the employee's election
business_rules.business_impact: If 401k were incorrectly included in COBRA processing, it would trigger inappropriate continuation coverage notices for a retirement account, confuse employees, and potentially mishandle retirement fund disposition. Conversely, if the separate 401k rollover/distribution process is not invoked, employees may face unintended tax consequences or loss of retirement savings portability rights under ERISA.

business_rules.title: COBRA Benefit End Date Aligns with the General Benefit End Date
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is being terminated and COBRA-eligible enrollments have been identified
business_rules.when_clause: The COBRA initiation process is triggered
business_rules.then_clause: The COBRA coverage start reference date must be the same end-of-month benefit end date calculated for all other terminating enrollments, ensuring continuity of coverage without gaps
business_rules.business_impact: If the COBRA benefit end date were misaligned with the general benefit termination date, employees could experience coverage gaps between their employer-sponsored plan ending and COBRA beginning, potentially leaving them uninsured for medical events and exposing the company to legal claims and regulatory scrutiny.

business_rules.title: 401k Employer Match Cap at 6% of Base Salary
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Benefits Administration / Compensation
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is enrolled in the 401k plan and has made an annual contribution
business_rules.when_clause: The employer 401k matching contribution is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: The matchable employee contribution amount is capped at 6% of the employee's base salary (EMPLOYER_401K_MATCH_CAP), meaning any employee contribution exceeding this threshold does not generate additional employer matching
business_rules.business_impact: If the cap is removed, the employer would match 50% of the employee's entire contribution regardless of salary, potentially resulting in unlimited and unbudgeted employer matching liability. This could significantly increase compensation costs and misrepresent the company's benefits policy to employees and auditors.

business_rules.title: Employer Matches 50% of Eligible Employee Contributions
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Benefits Administration / Compensation
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's matchable contribution amount has been determined (capped at 6% of base salary)
business_rules.when_clause: The employer 401k matching contribution is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: The employer contributes exactly 50% (EMPLOYER_401K_MATCH_PCT) of the matchable employee contribution amount, rounded to 2 decimal places
business_rules.business_impact: If the match percentage is removed or altered, the employer contribution would be incorrect — either over-paying employees (increasing cost) or under-paying (violating the stated benefits policy, reducing employee trust, and potentially creating legal or contractual obligations). Removing rounding could also cause financial reporting discrepancies.

business_rules.title: Employer Match Calculated on Base Salary Only
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Benefits Administration / Compensation
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has a defined base salary on record
business_rules.when_clause: The maximum matchable contribution ceiling is computed
business_rules.then_clause: Only the employee's base salary is used to determine the 6% cap, excluding bonuses, commissions, overtime, or other compensation components
business_rules.business_impact: If total compensation were used instead of base salary, employees with large variable pay components (bonuses, commissions) would receive higher employer matches than intended, increasing unbudgeted employer costs and creating inequitable treatment across employee compensation structures.

business_rules.title: Employer Match Rounded to Two Decimal Places (Currency Precision)
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Benefits Administration / Compensation
business_rules.given_clause: The employer 401k match amount has been computed as a floating-point value
business_rules.when_clause: The final employer match contribution is returned
business_rules.then_clause: The result is rounded to exactly 2 decimal places to conform to standard monetary precision
business_rules.business_impact: Without rounding, floating-point arithmetic could produce values with many decimal places (e.g., $1250.000000001), causing discrepancies in payroll systems, financial reporting, tax filings, and plan administrator records that expect standard currency precision.

business_rules.title: Standard 401k Annual Contribution IRS Limit
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Benefits Administration / Regulatory Compliance
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is enrolled in a 401k plan and has a proposed annual contribution amount
business_rules.when_clause: The proposed annual contribution amount is validated against the IRS statutory limit
business_rules.then_clause: The contribution must not exceed the standard IRS 401k annual contribution limit (RETIREMENT_401K_LIMIT); if it does, a ValueError is raised indicating the contribution exceeds the IRS limit
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, employees could contribute amounts exceeding IRS statutory limits, exposing the organization and employees to tax penalties, IRS audit risk, and potential plan disqualification. The employer could face significant legal and financial liability for facilitating non-compliant retirement contributions.

business_rules.title: Age-Based 401k Catch-Up Contribution Allowance for Employees 50 or Older
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Benefits Administration / Regulatory Compliance
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has a known date of birth and their calculated age is 50 years or older (>= RETIREMENT_401K_CATCHUP_AGE)
business_rules.when_clause: The IRS contribution limit is determined prior to validating the proposed annual contribution
business_rules.then_clause: The employee's allowable 401k contribution limit is increased by the IRS catch-up contribution amount (RETIREMENT_401K_CATCHUP), raising the effective ceiling above the standard limit
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, employees aged 50 and older would be incorrectly restricted to the standard contribution limit, denying them the IRS-sanctioned catch-up contribution benefit. This would disadvantage older employees in their retirement savings, potentially expose the organization to discrimination complaints, and fail to comply with IRS regulations that explicitly permit higher contributions for this age group.

business_rules.title: 401k Contribution Cannot Exceed Employee Annual Salary
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Benefits Administration / Regulatory Compliance
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has a base salary on record and a proposed annual 401k contribution amount
business_rules.when_clause: The proposed annual contribution amount is validated against the employee's base salary
business_rules.then_clause: The contribution must not exceed the employee's annual base salary; if it does, a ValueError is raised stating the contribution cannot exceed annual salary
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, an employee could theoretically elect a 401k contribution greater than their total compensation, which is economically nonsensical and likely violates IRS plan qualification rules (contributions must come from compensation). This could result in plan disqualification, payroll processing errors, negative net pay scenarios, and regulatory penalties.

business_rules.title: Catch-Up Contribution Eligibility Requires Known Date of Birth
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Benefits Administration / Regulatory Compliance
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's date of birth is not recorded (null/None)
business_rules.when_clause: The IRS contribution limit is determined prior to validating the proposed annual contribution
business_rules.then_clause: The age-based catch-up contribution allowance is not applied, and only the standard IRS limit is used for validation
business_rules.business_impact: If this guard were removed (i.e., catch-up always applied regardless of known DOB), employees without a recorded date of birth could receive an inflated contribution limit they may not be entitled to, risking IRS non-compliance. Conversely, if the guard were inverted to always deny catch-up when DOB is missing, eligible employees aged 50+ with incomplete records would be incorrectly penalized, creating a data quality dependency for regulatory benefit eligibility.

business_rules.title: Compa-Ratio Calculation Against Grade Band Midpoint
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Compensation & Pay Equity
business_rules.given_clause: An active employee has a base salary and a matching position record with a defined minimum and maximum salary range
business_rules.when_clause: A compensation report is generated
business_rules.then_clause: The employee's compa-ratio is calculated as their actual base salary divided by the midpoint of their grade band (midpoint = (min_salary + max_salary) / 2)
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, there is no standardised benchmark to assess whether employees are paid fairly relative to their grade band. Pay equity analysis becomes impossible, and the organisation loses the ability to identify systemic over- or under-payment patterns across roles and departments.

business_rules.title: Zero Midpoint Guard Defaults Compa-Ratio to Neutral
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Compensation & Pay Equity
business_rules.given_clause: An active employee has a matching position record where the computed grade band midpoint is zero
business_rules.when_clause: A compensation report is generated
business_rules.then_clause: The compa-ratio defaults to 1.0 (neutral/at-midpoint) to prevent a division-by-zero error and avoid false flagging of the employee
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, a zero midpoint would cause a runtime division-by-zero exception, crashing the report. If the default were changed to 0.0 instead of 1.0, employees with misconfigured grade bands would be incorrectly flagged as severely underpaid, generating false alerts and eroding trust in the compensation system.

business_rules.title: Underpayment Risk Flag at Compa-Ratio Below 0.80
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Compensation & Pay Equity
business_rules.given_clause: An active employee has a computed compa-ratio against their grade band midpoint
business_rules.when_clause: The compa-ratio is less than 0.80
business_rules.then_clause: The employee is flagged as an underpayment risk and included in the 'underpaid_employees' list with their employee ID, full name, actual salary, grade band midpoint, and rounded compa-ratio
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule eliminates the organisation's ability to proactively identify employees at risk of attrition due to below-market pay. It also exposes the company to pay equity litigation, as underpaid employees — who may disproportionately belong to protected groups — would go undetected and unaddressed.

business_rules.title: Overpayment or Salary Compression Flag at Compa-Ratio Above 1.20
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Compensation & Pay Equity
business_rules.given_clause: An active employee has a computed compa-ratio against their grade band midpoint
business_rules.when_clause: The compa-ratio is greater than 1.20
business_rules.then_clause: The employee is flagged for overpayment or salary compression and included in the 'overpaid_employees' list with their employee ID, full name, actual salary, grade band midpoint, and rounded compa-ratio
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, the organisation cannot detect salary compression (where senior employees earn only marginally more than junior ones) or budget overruns caused by salaries exceeding grade band ceilings. This leads to uncontrolled payroll costs and internal equity issues that can demotivate higher-performing employees paid within band.

business_rules.title: Only Active Employees Are Included in Compensation Analysis
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Compensation & Pay Equity
business_rules.given_clause: The organisation has both active and terminated employees in its data store
business_rules.when_clause: A compensation report is generated
business_rules.then_clause: Only active (non-terminated) employees are included in payroll totals, compa-ratio calculations, underpayment/overpayment flags, and department salary statistics
business_rules.business_impact: If terminated employees were included, total payroll figures, average salaries, and compa-ratio statistics would be inflated or distorted. Compensation decisions and budget forecasts based on this report would be materially inaccurate, and former employees could be incorrectly flagged for pay interventions.

business_rules.title: Employees Without a Matching Position Record Are Excluded from Compa-Ratio Analysis
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Compensation & Pay Equity
business_rules.given_clause: An active employee's job title and department do not match any position record in the system
business_rules.when_clause: A compensation report is generated
business_rules.then_clause: The employee's salary is still counted in total payroll and department statistics, but no compa-ratio is computed and they are not evaluated for underpayment or overpayment
business_rules.business_impact: Employees in roles without defined grade bands are silently excluded from pay equity analysis. This creates a blind spot — potentially a large one if many roles lack position records — meaning underpaid or overpaid employees in those roles are never flagged. It also skews the average compa-ratio by excluding unclassified employees.

business_rules.title: Average Compa-Ratio Defaults to Neutral When No Ratios Are Computed
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Compensation & Pay Equity
business_rules.given_clause: No active employees have a matching position record, resulting in an empty compa-ratio list
business_rules.when_clause: A compensation report is generated
business_rules.then_clause: The average compa-ratio is reported as 1.0 (neutral) rather than causing an error or reporting zero
business_rules.business_impact: If this defaulted to 0.0 instead of 1.0, stakeholders reviewing the report would see a misleading average compa-ratio suggesting the entire workforce is severely underpaid, triggering unnecessary and costly compensation reviews. A crash (division by zero) would prevent the report from being generated at all.

business_rules.title: Department-Level Salary Statistics Are Computed for All Active Employees
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Compensation & Pay Equity
business_rules.given_clause: Active employees are grouped by their department
business_rules.when_clause: A compensation report is generated
business_rules.then_clause: For each department, the report includes the headcount, average salary (rounded to 2 decimal places), minimum salary, and maximum salary across all active employees in that department
business_rules.business_impact: Without department-level statistics, HR and finance leaders cannot identify departments with anomalous salary distributions, cannot benchmark departments against each other, and lose the foundational data needed for gender pay gap analysis and budget planning at the organisational unit level.

business_rules.title: Department Identified by Name When Available, Falling Back to ID
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Compensation & Pay Equity
business_rules.given_clause: A department ID exists in the salary data but may or may not have a corresponding department record in the system
business_rules.when_clause: Department salary statistics are assembled for the report
business_rules.then_clause: The department is labelled by its human-readable name if a department record exists; otherwise the raw department ID is used as the label
business_rules.business_impact: If this fallback were removed and an unresolvable department ID caused an exception, the entire compensation report would fail to generate. If the fallback were omitted silently (e.g., skipping the department), salary data for employees in unmapped departments would be lost from the report, creating incomplete and misleading statistics.

business_rules.title: Average Salary Defaults to Zero When No Active Employees Exist
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Compensation & Pay Equity
business_rules.given_clause: There are no active employees in the organisation
business_rules.when_clause: A compensation report is generated
business_rules.then_clause: The reported average salary is 0 rather than causing a division-by-zero error
business_rules.business_impact: Without this guard, generating a compensation report for an organisation with no active employees (e.g., during system setup or after a mass termination event) would crash with a division-by-zero error, preventing any report output and potentially blocking downstream payroll or audit processes.

business_rules.title: New Department ID Is Mandatory for Transfer
business_rules.given_clause: An HR operator or system submits a POST /employees/{id}/transfer request
business_rules.when_clause: The request body does not include a 'new_department_id' field or the field is empty/null
business_rules.then_clause: The transfer is rejected with HTTP 400 and the message 'new_department_id is required', and no transfer is initiated
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, transfers could be submitted without specifying a destination department, resulting in employees being orphaned from any department, corrupting organisational structure data, and breaking downstream reporting, payroll allocation, and headcount management.

business_rules.title: Employee Must Be Active to Be Transferred
business_rules.given_clause: A transfer request is submitted for a specific employee identified by their ID
business_rules.when_clause: The employee service attempts to transfer the employee and detects the employee is not in an active status
business_rules.then_clause: A ValueError is raised by the employee service, caught by the handler, and an HTTP 400 error response is returned to the caller
business_rules.business_impact: If inactive, terminated, or on-leave employees could be transferred, it would create invalid organisational records, potentially reactivate terminated employees in the system, and undermine HR audit trails and compliance reporting.

business_rules.title: Employee Cannot Be Transferred While on Concurrent Leave
business_rules.given_clause: A transfer request is submitted for an employee who currently has an active leave record
business_rules.when_clause: The employee service checks for concurrent leave during the transfer operation and finds an active leave
business_rules.then_clause: A ValueError is raised by the employee service, caught by the handler, and an HTTP 400 error response is returned
business_rules.business_impact: Allowing transfers during active leave could create conflicting records between leave management and department assignment systems, cause payroll miscalculations, and violate HR policies that require leave to be resolved before organisational changes take effect.

business_rules.title: Target Department Must Have Available Headcount Capacity
business_rules.given_clause: A transfer request specifies a destination department
business_rules.when_clause: The employee service validates the target department's headcount capacity and finds it is at or over its approved limit
business_rules.then_clause: A ValueError is raised by the employee service, caught by the handler, and an HTTP 400 error response is returned
business_rules.business_impact: Without headcount capacity enforcement, departments could exceed their approved staffing budgets, violating workforce planning controls, financial budgets, and potentially breaching regulatory or contractual staffing limits.

business_rules.title: Salary Adjustment Must Fall Within New Grade Band
business_rules.given_clause: A transfer request optionally includes a new salary value
business_rules.when_clause: The employee service validates the proposed salary against the grade band of the new department/job title and finds it outside the permitted range
business_rules.then_clause: A ValueError is raised by the employee service, caught by the handler, and an HTTP 400 error response is returned
business_rules.business_impact: If salary adjustments are not validated against grade bands, employees could be transferred with salaries that violate compensation policy, creating pay equity issues, audit failures, and potential legal exposure under equal pay legislation.

business_rules.title: Job Title Update Is Optional During Transfer
business_rules.given_clause: A transfer request is submitted for an employee
business_rules.when_clause: The request body does not include a 'new_job_title' field
business_rules.then_clause: The transfer proceeds without changing the employee's job title, passing None to the employee service for that field
business_rules.business_impact: If job title were made mandatory, lateral transfers within the same role to a different department would be blocked unnecessarily, reducing HR flexibility and increasing administrative overhead for routine departmental reorganisations.

business_rules.title: Salary Update Is Optional During Transfer
business_rules.given_clause: A transfer request is submitted for an employee
business_rules.when_clause: The request body does not include a 'new_salary' field
business_rules.then_clause: The transfer proceeds without changing the employee's salary, passing None to the employee service for that field
business_rules.business_impact: If salary change were mandatory on every transfer, cost-neutral lateral moves would be blocked, forcing unnecessary compensation reviews and approvals for simple departmental reassignments, slowing down routine workforce management operations.

business_rules.title: Successful Transfer Must Be Confirmed with Transferred Status
business_rules.given_clause: All transfer validations pass and the employee record is successfully updated
business_rules.when_clause: The employee service completes the transfer operation
business_rules.then_clause: An HTTP 200 response is returned containing the employee's ID and a status of 'transferred', providing an auditable confirmation of the completed action
business_rules.business_impact: Without an explicit 'transferred' status confirmation, calling systems (e.g., workflow engines, notification services, audit logs) cannot reliably determine whether the transfer completed successfully, risking missed downstream actions such as access provisioning, equipment reassignment, or manager notifications.

business_rules.title: Prevent Duplicate Termination of Already-Terminated Employee
business_rules.given_clause: An employee record exists in the system with a status of TERMINATED
business_rules.when_clause: A termination request is submitted for that employee
business_rules.then_clause: The system raises a validation error and rejects the termination, preventing any duplicate offboarding workflows from being triggered
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, the same employee could be terminated multiple times, triggering duplicate COBRA notifications, multiple final paychecks, redundant access revocations, and corrupted HR records. This could result in financial overpayments, compliance violations, and data integrity issues in downstream systems.

business_rules.title: Involuntary Termination Requires Documented Reason
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is active and a termination request is being processed as involuntary (is_voluntary=False)
business_rules.when_clause: The termination is submitted without a documented reason
business_rules.then_clause: The system raises a validation error and blocks the termination until a reason is provided
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, involuntary terminations could be processed without justification, exposing the organisation to wrongful termination lawsuits, regulatory non-compliance (e.g., WARN Act, EEO documentation requirements), and inability to defend termination decisions in legal proceedings. It also undermines audit trails required by HR governance policies.

business_rules.title: Termination Date Defaults to Current Date if Not Specified
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is being terminated and no explicit termination date is provided
business_rules.when_clause: The termination is processed
business_rules.then_clause: The termination date is automatically set to today's date, ensuring the employee record always carries a valid termination date
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, termination records could have null termination dates, breaking payroll cutoff calculations, COBRA eligibility timelines (which are legally time-bound from the termination date), benefits end-date processing, and any downstream reporting or compliance filings that depend on a precise termination date.

business_rules.title: Pending Leave Requests Must Be Cancelled Upon Termination
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has pending or approved future leave requests in the system
business_rules.when_clause: The employee is terminated
business_rules.then_clause: All pending and approved future leave requests are cancelled as part of the offboarding workflow
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, terminated employees would retain open leave requests that could interfere with workforce planning, payroll accruals, and leave liability reporting. It could also create ghost leave approvals that confuse managers and distort headcount or absence analytics.

business_rules.title: Final Paycheck Must Be Triggered Upon Termination
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is being terminated
business_rules.when_clause: The termination is processed and the employee record is saved
business_rules.then_clause: A final paycheck workflow is triggered, including any owed PTO payout (subject to state law), ensuring the employee receives all legally owed compensation
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, terminated employees may not receive their final wages and owed PTO in a timely manner, violating state and federal wage payment laws (e.g., California Labor Code requires same-day final pay for involuntary terminations). This exposes the organisation to significant legal penalties, wage claims, and reputational damage.

business_rules.title: COBRA Eligibility Must Be Evaluated and Notified Upon Termination
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is being terminated
business_rules.when_clause: The termination is processed
business_rules.then_clause: The COBRA eligibility workflow is triggered, evaluating whether the employee qualifies (e.g., full-time status) and notifying the benefits team to issue the required COBRA election notice
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, the organisation would fail to provide mandatory COBRA continuation coverage notices as required by federal law (ERISA/COBRA regulations). Failure to notify within 14 days of the qualifying event can result in fines of up to $110 per day per qualified beneficiary, class-action exposure, and DOL investigations.

business_rules.title: System Access Must Be Revoked Upon Termination
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is being terminated
business_rules.when_clause: The termination is processed
business_rules.then_clause: All system and application access for the terminated employee is revoked as part of the offboarding workflow
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, terminated employees retain active credentials and system access, creating critical information security vulnerabilities. This violates SOX, HIPAA, SOC 2, and other compliance frameworks, and exposes the organisation to insider threats, data breaches, and regulatory penalties. It also violates the principle of least privilege and standard IT security policies.

business_rules.title: PTO Balance Payout Is State-Law Dependent Upon Termination
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is being terminated and has an outstanding PTO balance
business_rules.when_clause: The final paycheck workflow is triggered
business_rules.then_clause: The PTO payout is included in the final paycheck calculation in accordance with applicable state law (some states mandate payout, others do not)
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, the organisation may either fail to pay out PTO in states where it is legally required (e.g., California, Illinois) — resulting in wage claims and penalties — or incorrectly pay out PTO in states where it is not required, creating unnecessary financial liability. Proper state-law-dependent handling is essential for multi-state employers.

business_rules.title: Termination Reason Is Mandatory
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Employee Lifecycle / Offboarding
business_rules.given_clause: A request is made to terminate an employee's employment
business_rules.when_clause: The termination request body does not include a 'reason' field or the reason is empty/falsy
business_rules.then_clause: The termination is rejected with a 400 Bad Request error and the message 'Termination reason is required'; no offboarding workflow is initiated
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, terminations could be recorded with no documented reason, violating HR compliance requirements, audit trails, and potentially employment law obligations. This could expose the organisation to wrongful termination litigation, regulatory penalties, and inability to distinguish voluntary from involuntary separations for unemployment insurance and reporting purposes.

business_rules.title: Termination Voluntariness Classification
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Employee Lifecycle / Offboarding
business_rules.given_clause: A valid termination request is submitted with a reason
business_rules.when_clause: The request body does or does not include an 'is_voluntary' flag
business_rules.then_clause: The termination is classified as involuntary by default (is_voluntary=False) unless explicitly specified as voluntary; this classification is passed to the offboarding workflow
business_rules.business_impact: Voluntariness determines eligibility for unemployment insurance benefits, COBRA notification timelines, severance pay entitlements, and rehire eligibility. Defaulting incorrectly to involuntary could trigger unwarranted severance packages, affect unemployment insurance experience ratings, and misrepresent the separation type in regulatory filings (e.g., EEO-1, state unemployment reports).

business_rules.title: Full Offboarding Workflow Triggered on Valid Termination
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Employee Lifecycle / Offboarding
business_rules.given_clause: A termination request is submitted with a valid employee ID and a termination reason
business_rules.when_clause: The termination is processed successfully via the employee service
business_rules.then_clause: The full offboarding lifecycle is orchestrated, including benefit termination, COBRA eligibility processing, final paycheck generation, access revocation, and leave cancellation; the employee's status is set to 'terminated' and a success response is returned
business_rules.business_impact: If the offboarding workflow is not triggered atomically at termination, the organisation risks continued benefit costs for separated employees, missed COBRA notification deadlines (which carry statutory penalties under ERISA), delayed final paychecks (violating state wage payment laws), and active system access for former employees creating security and data privacy breaches.

business_rules.title: Invalid Termination Attempts Are Rejected with Business Error
business_rules.domain: Human Capital Management / Employee Lifecycle / Offboarding
business_rules.given_clause: A termination request is submitted for an employee
business_rules.when_clause: The employee service raises a ValueError during termination processing (e.g., employee not found, already terminated, or business rule violation within the service)
business_rules.then_clause: The termination is rejected with a 400 Bad Request error surfacing the specific business error message; no partial offboarding state is committed
business_rules.business_impact: Without this guard, invalid termination attempts (e.g., terminating an already-terminated employee, terminating a non-existent employee ID) could result in duplicate offboarding workflows, erroneous COBRA notices, duplicate final paychecks, or corrupted employee lifecycle state, leading to financial overpayments and compliance violations.

business_rules.title: Employee Must Exist
business_rules.given_clause: An employee ID is provided as part of a leave request submission
business_rules.when_clause: The system attempts to retrieve the employee record from the data store
business_rules.then_clause: If no employee record is found for the given ID, the request is rejected with an error indicating the employee does not exist
business_rules.business_impact: Without this check, leave requests could be submitted against non-existent employee IDs, corrupting leave records, causing downstream payroll or reporting errors, and potentially allowing fraudulent leave submissions.

business_rules.title: Only Active or Probationary Employees May Request Leave
business_rules.given_clause: An employee record exists in the system
business_rules.when_clause: A leave request is submitted for that employee
business_rules.then_clause: If the employee's employment status is neither ACTIVE nor PROBATION (e.g., terminated, suspended, retired), the request is rejected
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would allow terminated, suspended, or otherwise ineligible employees to submit leave requests, leading to fraudulent leave accrual, incorrect payroll deductions, and potential legal liability for the organisation.

business_rules.title: Leave Start Date Cannot Be in the Past
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is eligible to submit a leave request
business_rules.when_clause: A leave request is submitted with a start date earlier than today's date
business_rules.then_clause: The request is rejected with an error stating the start date cannot be in the past
