business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, employees could retroactively submit leave for days already worked, enabling backdating abuse, distorting attendance records, and undermining payroll accuracy and audit trails.

business_rules.title: Leave End Date Must Be On or After Start Date
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is submitting a leave request with a specified start date
business_rules.when_clause: The provided end date is earlier than the start date
business_rules.then_clause: The request is rejected with an error stating the end date must be on or after the start date
business_rules.business_impact: Without this constraint, logically invalid leave periods (negative duration) could be persisted, causing calculation errors in business-day counts, balance deductions, and reporting, and potentially crashing downstream processes.

business_rules.title: Probationary Employees Cannot Take PTO
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is currently in PROBATION status
business_rules.when_clause: A leave request is submitted with leave type PTO
business_rules.then_clause: The request is rejected with an error stating that probationary employees may only take sick leave, not PTO
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would allow probationary employees to consume PTO before they have earned it or before their employment is confirmed, violating HR policy, potentially breaching employment contracts, and creating unfair entitlement precedents.

business_rules.title: FMLA Leave Requires Federal Eligibility Criteria
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is submitting a leave request
business_rules.when_clause: The leave type is FMLA
business_rules.then_clause: The system validates that the employee meets federally mandated FMLA eligibility requirements (minimum 12 months of employment and 1,250 hours worked in the past 12 months); if not met, the request is rejected
business_rules.business_impact: Without this check, employees who do not qualify under the U.S. Family and Medical Leave Act could be granted FMLA-protected leave, exposing the organisation to legal risk, misuse of federally protected leave entitlements, and potential disputes with compliant employees who were denied leave.

business_rules.title: No Overlapping Approved Leave Periods
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is submitting a leave request for a specific date range
business_rules.when_clause: An existing approved or pending leave record for the same employee overlaps with the requested date range
business_rules.then_clause: The request is rejected with an error identifying the conflicting leave request ID and its date range
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, duplicate or overlapping leave requests could be approved, resulting in double-counting of leave days, incorrect balance deductions, payroll errors, and an inability to accurately track employee availability.

business_rules.title: Sufficient Leave Balance Required for PTO and Sick Leave
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is submitting a PTO or Sick leave request and a leave balance record exists (or is initialised) for the relevant year
business_rules.when_clause: The number of business days in the requested leave period exceeds the employee's available balance for that leave type
business_rules.then_clause: The request is rejected with an error specifying the leave type, the number of days requested, and the number of days available
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, employees could take more PTO or sick leave than they have accrued, leading to negative balances, payroll overpayments, policy violations, and potential financial loss for the organisation if leave is paid.

business_rules.title: Approved Leave Days Are Reserved (Pending) Upon Submission
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's PTO or Sick leave request passes all validation checks and a balance record exists
business_rules.when_clause: The leave request is successfully submitted
business_rules.then_clause: The number of business days in the request is added to the employee's pending_days balance and the updated balance is persisted, effectively reserving those days against future requests
business_rules.business_impact: Without this reservation step, multiple concurrent leave requests could each pass the balance check independently before any is persisted, allowing employees to over-commit their leave balance — a classic time-of-check/time-of-use race condition leading to negative balances.

business_rules.title: Only Pending Leave Requests Can Be Approved
business_rules.given_clause: A leave request exists in the system with a status other than PENDING (e.g., APPROVED, REJECTED, or CANCELLED)
business_rules.when_clause: An approver attempts to approve that leave request
business_rules.then_clause: The system raises a validation error indicating the request is not in a pending state and the approval is rejected
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, already-approved, rejected, or cancelled leave requests could be re-approved, causing duplicate approvals, incorrect leave balance deductions, conflicting records, and audit trail corruption. Employees could have leave reinstated after it was legitimately rejected or cancelled.

business_rules.title: Employee Cannot Approve Their Own Leave Request
business_rules.given_clause: A leave request is in PENDING status and the approver ID matches the employee ID on the request
business_rules.when_clause: The employee attempts to approve their own leave request
business_rules.then_clause: The system raises a validation error and prevents the self-approval
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, employees could unilaterally approve their own leave without managerial oversight, bypassing the entire approval chain. This would undermine workforce planning, enable abuse of leave entitlements, violate segregation-of-duties controls, and likely breach HR compliance and audit requirements.

business_rules.title: Approver Must Be the Employee's Direct Manager or an HR Admin
business_rules.given_clause: A leave request is in PENDING status, the approver is not the requesting employee, and the approver is neither the employee's manager nor an HR administrator
business_rules.when_clause: An unauthorised user attempts to approve the leave request
business_rules.then_clause: The system raises a validation error stating only the employee's manager or HR can approve leave, and the approval is denied
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, any user in the system could approve any employee's leave, completely breaking the organisational authority hierarchy. This would allow peers, subordinates, or unrelated staff to approve leave, undermining managerial accountability, workforce scheduling, and HR governance. It would also create significant compliance and audit risks.

business_rules.title: Leave Request Must Exist Before Approval Can Proceed
business_rules.given_clause: An approval action is initiated with a request ID that does not correspond to any existing leave request in the system
business_rules.when_clause: An approver attempts to approve the non-existent leave request
business_rules.then_clause: The system raises a validation error indicating the leave request was not found and no approval is processed
business_rules.business_impact: Without this guard, the system would attempt to operate on a null object, causing runtime errors. From a business perspective, approvals against non-existent requests could indicate data integrity issues, stale references, or attempts to manipulate the system with fabricated request IDs. Proper error surfacing ensures traceability and prevents silent failures.

business_rules.title: Approved Leave Request Is Stamped With Approver Identity and Approval Timestamp
business_rules.given_clause: A leave request has passed all validation checks and is eligible for approval
business_rules.when_clause: The approval action is successfully executed
business_rules.then_clause: The leave request status is set to APPROVED, the approver's ID is recorded on the request, and the current UTC timestamp is captured as the approval time, and the updated record is persisted
business_rules.business_impact: Without recording the approver identity and timestamp, the organisation loses the audit trail required for HR compliance, payroll processing, and dispute resolution. It would be impossible to determine who approved a leave request or when, violating accountability principles and potentially breaching labour law record-keeping obligations.

business_rules.title: Leave Request Must Exist Before Denial
business_rules.given_clause: A leave request denial is being processed for a given request ID
business_rules.when_clause: The system attempts to retrieve the leave request and it does not exist in the data store
business_rules.then_clause: The denial is rejected with an error indicating the leave request was not found
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, denial operations could silently fail or corrupt state against non-existent records, leading to phantom approver actions, audit trail inconsistencies, and potential data integrity issues in the leave management system.

business_rules.title: Only Pending Leave Requests Can Be Denied
business_rules.given_clause: A leave request exists in the system
business_rules.when_clause: An approver attempts to deny a leave request that is not in PENDING status (e.g., already approved, denied, or cancelled)
business_rules.then_clause: The denial is rejected with an error indicating the request is not in a pending state
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would allow approvers to deny already-approved, previously-denied, or cancelled leave requests. This could cause double-release of reserved balances, incorrect leave balance calculations, employee confusion, and audit/compliance violations where the same request is actioned multiple times.

business_rules.title: Denial Reason Is Mandatory
business_rules.given_clause: An approver is denying a pending leave request
business_rules.when_clause: The denial is submitted without a denial reason (empty string, None, or falsy value)
business_rules.then_clause: The denial is rejected with an error stating that a denial reason is required
business_rules.business_impact: Without a mandatory denial reason, employees would receive no explanation for why their leave was denied, violating transparency and fairness obligations. This could expose the organisation to HR disputes, legal challenges, and non-compliance with labour regulations that require documented justification for leave denials.

business_rules.title: Denied Leave Request Records the Approver Identity
business_rules.given_clause: A pending leave request is being denied with a valid denial reason
business_rules.when_clause: The denial is processed and the request status is updated to DENIED
business_rules.then_clause: The approver's ID is recorded on the leave request to establish accountability and audit trail
business_rules.business_impact: Without recording the approver ID, there would be no accountability trail for who denied a leave request. This undermines audit compliance, makes it impossible to investigate disputed denials, and removes managerial accountability required by most HR governance frameworks.

business_rules.title: Denial Reason Is Persisted on the Leave Request
business_rules.given_clause: A pending leave request is being denied
business_rules.when_clause: The denial is processed with a valid denial reason
business_rules.then_clause: The denial reason is stored on the leave request record for future reference and employee communication
business_rules.business_impact: If the denial reason is not persisted, employees and HR cannot review why a request was denied after the fact. This breaks employee self-service portals, appeal processes, and compliance reporting, and may violate labour law requirements to provide documented reasons for leave refusals.

business_rules.title: Reserved PTO and Sick Leave Balance Must Be Released Upon Denial
business_rules.given_clause: A pending leave request of type PTO or SICK has been denied, and the employee's leave balance has pending days reserved for that request
business_rules.when_clause: The leave request denial is finalised
business_rules.then_clause: The number of business days previously reserved (pending_days) for the denied request is released back to the employee's available leave balance
business_rules.business_impact: Without releasing reserved balance on denial, employees would permanently lose leave days that were never actually taken. This would incorrectly deplete PTO/sick entitlements, cause employee dissatisfaction, trigger payroll errors, and potentially violate statutory entitlement obligations requiring accurate leave balance tracking.

business_rules.title: Released Pending Days Cannot Fall Below Zero
business_rules.given_clause: A denied leave request triggers a release of reserved balance days for a PTO or SICK leave type
business_rules.when_clause: The pending days calculation would result in a negative value (e.g., due to data inconsistency)
business_rules.then_clause: The pending days balance is floored at zero to prevent negative leave balance states
business_rules.business_impact: Without this floor, data inconsistencies or race conditions could produce negative pending day counts, corrupting leave balance records. This could cause incorrect entitlement calculations, erroneous payroll deductions, and misleading leave balance displays to employees and HR managers.

business_rules.title: Balance Release Only Applies to PTO and Sick Leave Types
business_rules.given_clause: A leave request of any leave type is being denied
business_rules.when_clause: The denial is processed and balance release logic is evaluated
business_rules.then_clause: Reserved balance is only released for PTO and SICK leave types; other leave types (e.g., unpaid, bereavement) do not trigger a balance adjustment
business_rules.business_impact: If balance release were applied to all leave types indiscriminately, leave types without tracked balances (e.g., unpaid leave, bereavement) could have phantom balance records created or corrupted. Conversely, if PTO/SICK were excluded, employees would permanently lose accrued entitlement days, causing financial liability and compliance failures.

business_rules.title: Only Requesting Employee Can Cancel Leave
business_rules.given_clause: A leave request exists and an employee attempts to cancel it
business_rules.when_clause: The employee_id provided does not match the employee_id on the leave request
business_rules.then_clause: The cancellation is rejected with an error stating only the requesting employee can cancel their leave
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, any employee or system actor could cancel another employee's leave, enabling unauthorised interference with colleagues' approved time off, potential HR disputes, and loss of audit integrity around who initiated cancellations.

business_rules.title: Cannot Cancel In-Progress or Completed Leave
business_rules.given_clause: A leave request exists with a status of IN_PROGRESS or COMPLETED
business_rules.when_clause: The requesting employee attempts to cancel the leave
business_rules.then_clause: The cancellation is rejected with an error indicating the leave cannot be cancelled in its current state
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, employees could retroactively cancel leave that has already started or finished, leading to incorrect balance recalculations, payroll discrepancies, and falsified attendance records.

business_rules.title: Cannot Cancel Already-Cancelled Leave
business_rules.given_clause: A leave request exists with a status of CANCELLED
business_rules.when_clause: The requesting employee attempts to cancel the leave again
business_rules.then_clause: The cancellation is rejected with an error stating the leave is already cancelled
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, duplicate cancellation operations could trigger repeated manager notifications, redundant balance adjustments, and corrupt the leave request lifecycle state, undermining data integrity.

business_rules.title: Manager Notification Required for Late Cancellation of Approved Leave
business_rules.given_clause: A leave request has been approved and the start date is less than or equal to 1 day away (i.e., within 24 hours)
business_rules.when_clause: The requesting employee cancels the approved leave
business_rules.then_clause: A manager notification is triggered to alert them of the late cancellation before the status is updated
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, managers would have no visibility into last-minute leave cancellations, making it impossible to adjust staffing plans, reassign coverage, or address potential attendance issues in time, leading to operational disruption.

business_rules.title: Release Pending Leave Balance on Cancellation for PTO and Sick Leave
business_rules.given_clause: A leave request of type PTO or SICK is being cancelled
business_rules.when_clause: The cancellation is successfully processed
business_rules.then_clause: The employee's pending_days balance for the relevant leave type and year is reduced by the number of business days in the cancelled request (floored at zero)
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, cancelled leave would continue to hold days in a pending state, artificially reducing the employee's available leave balance. Employees would be unable to book new leave against entitlement they are rightfully owed, causing incorrect balance reporting and potential employee grievances.

business_rules.title: Leave Cancellation Only Applies to Pending or Approved Requests
business_rules.given_clause: A leave request exists in any status
business_rules.when_clause: The requesting employee attempts to cancel the leave
business_rules.then_clause: Only requests with a status of PENDING or APPROVED are eligible for cancellation; all other statuses (IN_PROGRESS, COMPLETED, CANCELLED) are explicitly rejected
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, the leave lifecycle would have no enforced state machine, allowing cancellations at any stage and breaking downstream processes such as payroll integration, attendance tracking, and leave balance management that depend on valid state transitions.

business_rules.title: Default Leave Balance Year to Current Calendar Year
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's leave balance is being requested for a specific leave type, and no year is explicitly provided by the caller
business_rules.when_clause: The get_leave_balance function is invoked without a year argument (year is None or omitted)
business_rules.then_clause: The system defaults the query year to the current calendar year (as determined by today's date), ensuring the balance retrieved or initialised corresponds to the active leave period
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, callers omitting the year would receive an error or retrieve an incorrect/undefined balance. Employees and HR systems relying on the default behaviour would see failures or incorrect leave entitlements, potentially allowing or blocking leave requests based on wrong-year data. Year-end carryover processes and accrual calculations could also be disrupted if balances are initialised against the wrong year.

business_rules.title: Guarantee Existence of Leave Balance Record for Employee, Type, and Year
business_rules.given_clause: An employee ID, a leave type, and a resolved year (either explicitly provided or defaulted to the current year) are available
business_rules.when_clause: The get_leave_balance function is called to retrieve the employee's leave balance
business_rules.then_clause: The system either returns the existing leave balance record or automatically initialises and persists a new one with the correct entitlement based on the leave type and applicable accrual rules, ensuring a balance record always exists after the call
business_rules.business_impact: If this rule were removed and the function only retrieved (but never created) balances, employees without a pre-existing record would receive errors or null results when checking their leave balance. This would block leave requests, prevent accrual initialisation, and require a separate manual or batch process to seed balance records — introducing operational gaps, especially for new employees or new leave types being introduced mid-year.

business_rules.title: Root Employee Must Exist Before Org Tree Can Be Built
business_rules.given_clause: A request is made to construct an organisational hierarchy tree starting from a specific employee
business_rules.when_clause: The employee ID provided as the root node does not correspond to any existing employee record in the data store
business_rules.then_clause: The system raises a standardised error and does not return any organisational tree data
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, org trees could be constructed from non-existent or invalid employee anchors, producing phantom hierarchies, corrupting reporting structure data, and potentially exposing unintended parts of the organisation chart to unauthorised requestors.

business_rules.title: Organisational Hierarchy Depth Is Bounded By A Configurable Maximum
business_rules.given_clause: A valid root employee exists and an org tree construction is initiated
business_rules.when_clause: The recursive traversal of the reporting structure is executed
business_rules.then_clause: The tree expansion is limited to a maximum depth (defaulting to 5 levels), preventing unbounded traversal of the reporting chain
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, deeply nested or circular reporting structures could cause infinite recursion or extremely large response payloads, making the org chart unusable, degrading system performance, and potentially crashing the service. The default of 5 also encodes an implicit organisational policy assumption about the maximum meaningful reporting depth to display.

business_rules.title: Default Organisational View Spans Five Reporting Levels
business_rules.given_clause: A caller requests an org tree without specifying an explicit depth limit
business_rules.when_clause: The org tree is constructed using the default configuration
business_rules.then_clause: The hierarchy is rendered up to 5 levels deep from the root employee, representing the standard organisational visibility policy
business_rules.business_impact: If the default were changed or removed, the standard org chart view would either show too little (reducing managerial visibility) or too much (exposing deep sub-structures unnecessarily, increasing data exposure risk and response payload size), breaking the implicit organisational reporting visibility policy.

business_rules.title: Org Tree Is Rooted At A Named Employee, Not At The Organisation Top
business_rules.given_clause: The organisation has a full reporting hierarchy
business_rules.when_clause: An org tree is requested for a specific employee ID
business_rules.then_clause: The returned hierarchy is scoped exclusively to that employee as the root, representing only their downward reporting chain rather than the full company structure
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or altered to always start from the top of the organisation, managers would lose the ability to view scoped sub-trees of their own teams, violating least-privilege data visibility principles and potentially exposing the entire company hierarchy to any employee who requests an org chart.

business_rules.title: Turnover Rate Formula: Separations Over Average Headcount
business_rules.given_clause: A reporting period with a known average headcount and a set of employee terminations
business_rules.when_clause: The turnover report is calculated for the given period
business_rules.then_clause: The overall turnover rate is computed as (total separations / average headcount) × 100, rounded to one decimal place
business_rules.business_impact: Removing or altering this formula would produce incorrect turnover metrics, making workforce analytics unreliable and incomparable to industry benchmarks. Decisions on retention investment and staffing strategy would be based on flawed data.

business_rules.title: Zero Headcount Guard: Prevent Division by Zero
business_rules.given_clause: A reporting period where the average headcount is zero (e.g., a newly formed or dissolved organisation unit)
business_rules.when_clause: The turnover rate is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: All turnover rates (overall, voluntary, involuntary) default to 0 rather than causing a division-by-zero error
business_rules.business_impact: Without this guard, a zero headcount would cause a runtime exception, crashing the report. Returning 0 is also a deliberate business choice — an undefined rate is represented as 0%, which could mislead analysts if not documented, but prevents system failure.

business_rules.title: Separation Classification: Voluntary vs Involuntary
business_rules.given_clause: A list of termination records, each carrying an 'is_voluntary' flag
business_rules.when_clause: The turnover report is generated
business_rules.then_clause: Each termination is classified as either voluntary (is_voluntary = true) or involuntary (is_voluntary = false or absent), and separate counts and rates are reported for each category
business_rules.business_impact: Without this classification, HR cannot distinguish between regrettable attrition (voluntary) and workforce restructuring (involuntary). Retention programmes, exit interview policies, and legal compliance reporting all depend on this distinction. Merging the two categories would obscure root causes of turnover.

business_rules.title: Missing is_voluntary Flag Treated as Involuntary
business_rules.given_clause: A termination record where the 'is_voluntary' field is absent or null
business_rules.when_clause: The termination is classified into voluntary or involuntary buckets
business_rules.then_clause: The termination is counted as involuntary because the falsy check (not t.get('is_voluntary')) captures both False and None/missing values
business_rules.business_impact: If data quality is poor and many records lack the flag, involuntary turnover will be overstated and voluntary turnover understated. This could trigger unnecessary management concern about layoffs or legal exposure, and suppress focus on genuine voluntary attrition drivers.

business_rules.title: Department-Level Turnover Breakdown for Targeted Retention
business_rules.given_clause: Termination records that include a 'department' field
business_rules.when_clause: The turnover report is generated
business_rules.then_clause: Separations are aggregated by department, with counts of total, voluntary, and involuntary terminations per department reported separately
business_rules.business_impact: Without department-level breakdown, HR and business leaders cannot identify which teams have disproportionately high turnover. Retention initiatives would be applied uniformly rather than targeted, wasting resources and failing to address root causes in high-risk departments.

business_rules.title: Unknown Department Fallback for Missing Department Data
business_rules.given_clause: A termination record where the 'department' field is absent or null
business_rules.when_clause: The department breakdown is computed
business_rules.then_clause: The termination is attributed to a department labelled 'Unknown' rather than being dropped or causing an error
business_rules.business_impact: Without this fallback, records with missing department data would be silently excluded from the breakdown, causing total separations in the department view to not reconcile with the overall total. Analysts would receive inconsistent reports and might make incorrect staffing decisions.

business_rules.title: Tenure-at-Separation Bucketing into Five Standard Bands
business_rules.given_clause: Termination records that include a 'tenure_years' field representing years of service at the time of separation
business_rules.when_clause: The turnover report is generated
business_rules.then_clause: Each termination is assigned to exactly one of five tenure bands: less than 1 year, 1–3 years, 3–5 years, 5–10 years, or 10 or more years
business_rules.business_impact: Without tenure bucketing, HR cannot identify whether turnover is concentrated among new hires (onboarding failure), mid-tenure employees (engagement issues), or long-tenured staff (retirement or burnout). Each band requires a different retention or succession strategy. Removing this analysis would blind the organisation to lifecycle-stage attrition patterns.

business_rules.title: Missing Tenure Defaults to Zero (Less Than One Year Band)
business_rules.given_clause: A termination record where the 'tenure_years' field is absent or null
business_rules.when_clause: The tenure bucket is determined for that termination
business_rules.then_clause: The tenure is treated as 0, placing the termination in the '<1yr' bucket
business_rules.business_impact: If many records lack tenure data, the '<1yr' bucket will be artificially inflated, potentially triggering false alarms about new-hire attrition and misdirecting onboarding improvement efforts. Data quality controls should accompany this default.

business_rules.title: Turnover Rates Rounded to One Decimal Place
business_rules.given_clause: Computed floating-point turnover rates for overall, voluntary, and involuntary categories
business_rules.when_clause: The turnover report result is assembled
business_rules.then_clause: Each rate is rounded to one decimal place before being included in the report output
business_rules.business_impact: Without rounding, rates would be reported with many decimal places, reducing readability in dashboards and executive reports. Inconsistent precision could also cause minor discrepancies when rates are summed or compared across periods, undermining trust in the data.

business_rules.title: Report Period Labelled as Inclusive Date Range String
business_rules.given_clause: A start date and end date defining the reporting window
business_rules.when_clause: The turnover report result is assembled
business_rules.then_clause: The period is represented as a human-readable string in the format 'start_date to end_date' and included in the report output
business_rules.business_impact: Without an explicit period label, consumers of the report (dashboards, downstream systems, printed summaries) cannot determine which time window the data covers, risking misinterpretation or incorrect period-over-period comparisons.

business_rules.title: Retirement Eligibility Age Threshold at 60+
business_rules.given_clause: An active employee has a recorded date of birth and workforce planning metrics are being computed
business_rules.when_clause: The employee's calculated age is greater than or equal to 60 years
business_rules.then_clause: The employee is flagged as retirement-eligible within the 5-year retirement risk window (approaching the standard retirement age of 65)
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or the threshold is changed, HR would lose visibility into near-term retirement risk. Succession planning, knowledge transfer initiatives, and workforce gap analysis would be compromised, potentially leaving critical roles unfilled when experienced employees retire unexpectedly.

business_rules.title: Retirement Risk Scope Limited to Active Employees Only
business_rules.given_clause: The workforce planning metrics computation is triggered
business_rules.when_clause: The system retrieves the employee population for analysis
business_rules.then_clause: Only currently active (non-terminated) employees are included in all workforce planning metrics, including retirement eligibility, span of control, and tenure calculations
business_rules.business_impact: If terminated employees were included, all metrics would be inflated or distorted — retirement risk counts would be overstated, span of control would be miscalculated, and average tenure would be skewed, leading to flawed strategic HR decisions.

business_rules.title: Employees Without Date of Birth Are Excluded from Retirement Risk
business_rules.given_clause: An active employee record exists in the system
business_rules.when_clause: Retirement eligibility is being assessed and the employee's date_of_birth field is null or missing
business_rules.then_clause: The employee is silently excluded from the retirement eligibility list and does not contribute to the retirement risk count
business_rules.business_impact: Employees with missing birth dates are invisible to retirement risk planning. If many records lack this field, the retirement risk metric will be significantly understated, causing HR to under-prepare for succession needs. Conversely, removing this guard could cause runtime errors.

business_rules.title: Age Calculation Uses Precise Calendar-Accurate Day Division (365.25)
business_rules.given_clause: An active employee has a valid date of birth
business_rules.when_clause: The system calculates the employee's current age for retirement eligibility assessment
business_rules.then_clause: Age is computed as the number of elapsed days divided by 365.25 to account for leap years, ensuring actuarially accurate age determination
business_rules.business_impact: Using 365 instead of 365.25 would introduce a small but cumulative error (~0.07% per year), potentially misclassifying employees near the exact 60-year boundary. Over a large workforce, this could cause employees to be incorrectly included or excluded from retirement risk reporting.

business_rules.title: Span of Control Calculated Only for Employees with an Assigned Manager
business_rules.given_clause: Active employees are being analyzed for span of control metrics
business_rules.when_clause: An employee record is evaluated for manager assignment
business_rules.then_clause: Only employees who have a non-null manager_id contribute to the manager's direct report count, effectively excluding top-level executives or unassigned employees from the denominator
business_rules.business_impact: If employees without a manager_id were included (e.g., counted under a default manager), the span of control metric would be artificially inflated or distorted. Removing this filter could misrepresent management load, leading to incorrect decisions about management structure or hiring of additional managers.

business_rules.title: Average Span of Control Defaults to Zero When No Manager Relationships Exist
business_rules.given_clause: Workforce planning metrics are being computed
business_rules.when_clause: No active employees have an assigned manager_id, resulting in an empty manager_counts dictionary
business_rules.then_clause: The average span of control is reported as 0 rather than causing a division-by-zero error
business_rules.business_impact: Primarily a technical safeguard, but the business implication is that a zero value in the report signals an absence of formal reporting relationships, which could indicate a data quality issue or an org structure anomaly that HR should investigate.

business_rules.title: Average Tenure Defaults to Zero When No Employee Tenure Data Exists
business_rules.given_clause: Workforce planning metrics are being computed
business_rules.when_clause: The active employee list is empty or no tenure values are available
business_rules.then_clause: The average tenure is reported as 0 rather than causing a division-by-zero error
business_rules.business_impact: Primarily a technical safeguard. A reported tenure of 0 in a real organization would be a strong signal of a data integrity problem. If this guard were removed, the system would crash on empty datasets, preventing any workforce planning report from being generated.

business_rules.title: Retirement Risk Detail Report Capped at Top 10 Employees
business_rules.given_clause: One or more active employees have been identified as retirement-eligible (age 60+)
business_rules.when_clause: The workforce planning metrics result is assembled for return
business_rules.then_clause: The detailed retirement risk employee list included in the output is limited to a maximum of 10 employees, while the full count is still reported separately
business_rules.business_impact: If this cap were removed, the API response payload could become extremely large in organizations with many near-retirement employees, potentially causing performance issues or overwhelming downstream consumers (e.g., dashboards). The full count metric ensures the total risk exposure is still accurately communicated regardless of the detail cap.

business_rules.title: Retirement Risk Age Reported with One Decimal Precision
business_rules.given_clause: An active employee is identified as retirement-eligible
business_rules.when_clause: The employee's age is included in the retirement risk detail record
business_rules.then_clause: The age value is rounded to one decimal place for reporting purposes
business_rules.business_impact: Minor presentational rule. If removed, ages would be reported with full floating-point precision (e.g., 62.847945...), making reports harder to read. Standardizing to one decimal place ensures consistent, human-readable output for HR stakeholders reviewing retirement risk.

business_rules.title: COBRA Eligibility Restricted to Full-Time Employees
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is being processed for termination and the COBRA eligibility check is triggered
business_rules.when_clause: The employee's employment type is evaluated during the offboarding workflow
business_rules.then_clause: COBRA continuation coverage eligibility is only triggered and the benefits team is notified if the employee is classified as full-time; part-time, contract, or other employment types are excluded
business_rules.business_impact: If this rule is removed or inverted: (1) Part-time, temporary, or contract employees could incorrectly receive COBRA notices, creating legal liability and administrative overhead for ineligible individuals. (2) If inverted, full-time employees would be denied COBRA notification, violating federal COBRA law (ERISA/IRS regulations) which mandates that qualifying beneficiaries — typically full-time employees on group health plans — receive timely continuation coverage notices upon a qualifying event such as termination. Non-compliance can result in IRS excise tax penalties of up to $100 per day per qualified beneficiary and potential DOL enforcement actions.

business_rules.title: Full-Time Employment Required for Benefits Eligibility
business_rules.given_clause: An employee exists in the HR system with a defined employment type
business_rules.when_clause: A benefits eligibility check is performed on the employee
business_rules.then_clause: The employee is deemed ineligible for benefits if their employment type is not FULL_TIME (e.g., part-time, contractor, or temporary employees are excluded)
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, part-time, temporary, or contract employees would become eligible for benefits enrollment, significantly increasing the company's benefits cost burden, creating compliance risks with benefits plan documents, and potentially violating ERISA plan terms or insurance carrier agreements that restrict coverage to full-time employees.

business_rules.title: Terminated Employees Are Ineligible for Benefits
business_rules.given_clause: An employee exists in the HR system with an employment status of TERMINATED
business_rules.when_clause: A benefits eligibility check is performed on the employee
business_rules.then_clause: The employee is deemed ineligible for benefits enrollment regardless of their employment type or tenure
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, terminated employees could be enrolled in or continue to receive company-sponsored benefits after separation, exposing the company to unauthorized benefit costs, potential fraud, COBRA/HIPAA compliance violations, and legal liability for covering individuals no longer employed by the organization.

business_rules.title: 90-Day Probationary Period Must Be Completed for Benefits Eligibility
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is full-time and has a non-terminated employment status
business_rules.when_clause: A benefits eligibility check is performed on the employee
business_rules.then_clause: The employee is eligible for benefits only if their tenure is greater than or equal to 90 days (approximately 0.2464 years, calculated as 90 / 365.25 to account for leap years)
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, newly hired employees could enroll in benefits immediately upon hire, increasing costs for employees who may not complete their probationary period, undermining the company's workforce commitment policy, and potentially conflicting with benefits plan documents or insurance carrier waiting-period requirements mandated under ACA regulations.

business_rules.title: Onboarding Workflow Initiated Upon Employee Hire
business_rules.given_clause: A new employee has been successfully hired and persisted in the system with a valid employee_id
business_rules.when_clause: The onboarding process is triggered for the newly hired employee
business_rules.then_clause: A set of onboarding tasks (IT setup, badge provisioning, orientation scheduling, etc.) must be created for that employee in the system
business_rules.business_impact: If this rule is removed, new employees would not have onboarding tasks created, resulting in missing IT access, no security badge, and no orientation scheduling. This would leave new hires unable to perform their job duties from day one, creating operational disruption, security gaps (unprovisioned access), and a poor employee experience. Compliance obligations around structured onboarding may also be violated.

business_rules.title: Onboarding Tasks Are Scoped to a Specific Employee Identity
business_rules.given_clause: An employee record exists with a unique employee_id
business_rules.when_clause: Onboarding tasks are created
business_rules.then_clause: All onboarding tasks must be associated with the specific employee's unique identifier (employee_id), not a name or other attribute
business_rules.business_impact: If tasks were associated by a non-unique identifier (e.g., name), onboarding tasks could be misassigned to the wrong employee, causing incorrect IT provisioning, badge issuance to the wrong person, or duplicate/missing tasks for employees with similar names. This creates security and compliance risks.

business_rules.title: Onboarding Is a Mandatory Step in the Hiring Lifecycle
business_rules.given_clause: The hire_employee orchestration has completed department capacity validation, salary grade validation, employment status determination, and employee record persistence
business_rules.when_clause: The final step of the hire_employee workflow executes
business_rules.then_clause: Onboarding must always be triggered as a non-optional concluding action of the hiring process
business_rules.business_impact: If onboarding is made optional or skipped, newly hired employees may start without necessary system access, physical access credentials, or structured orientation. This undermines HR policy compliance, delays employee productivity, and may violate labor or security regulations requiring documented onboarding procedures.

business_rules.title: Active Employee Tenure Uses Current Date
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has a hire date but no termination date (i.e., is currently active)
business_rules.when_clause: The employee's tenure in years is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: Today's date is used as the end date for the tenure calculation, reflecting continuous service up to the present moment
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or inverted, active employees would have no valid end date for tenure calculation, causing errors or incorrect tenure values. Compensation decisions, benefits eligibility, and service awards based on tenure would be miscalculated for all currently employed staff.

business_rules.title: Terminated Employee Tenure Uses Termination Date
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has both a hire date and a termination date
business_rules.when_clause: The employee's tenure in years is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: The termination date is used as the end date, so tenure reflects only the actual period of employment and does not accrue beyond the last day of service
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, terminated employees' tenure would continue to grow beyond their last day of service, inflating severance calculations, rehire eligibility assessments, and historical reporting. This could result in financial overpayments or incorrect HR records.

business_rules.title: Tenure Calculated in Calendar Years Using 365.25-Day Year
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's start and end dates are known
business_rules.when_clause: The tenure duration in years is computed from the day difference
business_rules.then_clause: The total elapsed days are divided by 365.25 to account for leap years, producing an accurate fractional year value
business_rules.business_impact: If a simpler divisor (e.g., 365) were used instead, tenure would be slightly overstated over long periods, potentially triggering milestone benefits (e.g., 5-year or 10-year awards) prematurely. Using 366 would understate tenure, potentially delaying entitlements unfairly.

business_rules.title: Tenure Rounded to One Decimal Place
business_rules.given_clause: A raw fractional year value has been computed for an employee's tenure
business_rules.when_clause: The tenure value is returned to the caller
business_rules.then_clause: The value is rounded to exactly one decimal place (e.g., 3.47 becomes 3.5), providing a standardised level of precision for HR reporting and policy application
business_rules.business_impact: If rounding were removed or changed to a different precision, tenure-based thresholds (e.g., eligibility at 0.5-year increments) could be evaluated inconsistently across systems. Displaying raw floating-point values in reports would reduce readability and could cause boundary disputes in policy enforcement.

business_rules.title: Revoke All System Accounts on Employee Termination
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has been terminated and their employee_id is provided to the offboarding workflow
business_rules.when_clause: The system access revocation step is triggered as part of the termination lifecycle
business_rules.then_clause: All system accounts associated with the terminated employee must be disabled
business_rules.business_impact: If this rule is removed or not enforced, terminated employees could retain active system credentials, creating significant security vulnerabilities including unauthorized data access, intellectual property theft, sabotage, or compliance violations (e.g., SOX, HIPAA, GDPR). This is a critical offboarding security control.

business_rules.title: Revoke Physical Badge Access on Employee Termination
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has been terminated and their employee_id is provided to the offboarding workflow
business_rules.when_clause: The system access revocation step is triggered as part of the termination lifecycle
business_rules.then_clause: Physical badge/facility access for the terminated employee must be revoked
business_rules.business_impact: If badge access revocation is skipped, a terminated employee could physically enter secured facilities, posing risks of theft, workplace violence, data breach, or unauthorized access to restricted areas. This also creates liability and may violate physical security compliance requirements.

business_rules.title: Access Revocation Must Be Audited and Logged
business_rules.given_clause: A system access revocation action is performed for a terminated employee
business_rules.when_clause: The revocation process executes
business_rules.then_clause: An audit log entry must be recorded confirming that system access was revoked for the specific employee_id
business_rules.business_impact: Without an audit trail of access revocation, the organization cannot demonstrate compliance with security policies or regulatory requirements (e.g., SOX, ISO 27001). In the event of a security incident involving a former employee, the absence of logs would impede forensic investigation and could expose the organization to legal and regulatory penalties.

business_rules.title: Access Revocation Is Mandatory Step in Termination Lifecycle
business_rules.given_clause: An employee termination has been initiated via terminate_employee
business_rules.when_clause: The full offboarding workflow is orchestrated
business_rules.then_clause: System and badge access revocation must be executed as a non-optional downstream step before the termination process is considered complete
business_rules.business_impact: If access revocation is made optional or removed from the termination lifecycle, inconsistent offboarding could leave some terminated employees with active access. This creates unpredictable security posture, audit failures, and potential insider threat scenarios. Regulatory frameworks typically mandate timely access termination upon employment end.

business_rules.title: Weekdays Only Count as Business Days
business_rules.given_clause: A leave request has a defined start_date and end_date range
business_rules.when_clause: The number of business days in the leave period is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: Only days with weekday() values 0 through 4 (Monday to Friday) are counted; Saturday (5) and Sunday (6) are excluded from the total
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or inverted, weekends would be counted as working days, causing employees to be charged leave for non-working days (Saturday/Sunday). This would unfairly reduce employees' leave balances, lead to payroll and compliance errors, and violate standard labour regulations that define business days as Monday–Friday.

business_rules.title: Inclusive Date Range for Leave Period
business_rules.given_clause: A leave request has a start_date and an end_date
business_rules.when_clause: The business day count is computed by iterating through the date range
business_rules.then_clause: Both the start_date and end_date are included in the count (loop condition is current <= self.end_date), meaning a single-day leave request on a weekday yields exactly 1 business day
business_rules.business_impact: If the end_date were excluded (strict less-than), a one-day leave request would return 0 business days, making it impossible to book single-day leave and causing systematic under-counting of leave duration across all requests. This would corrupt leave balance deductions and reporting.

business_rules.title: Business Day Calculation Spans Full Calendar Range
business_rules.given_clause: A leave request with a start_date and end_date spanning multiple calendar weeks
business_rules.when_clause: Business days are counted by iterating day-by-day from start_date to end_date
business_rules.then_clause: Every calendar day in the range is individually evaluated, ensuring weekends embedded within a multi-week leave period are correctly excluded regardless of the range length
business_rules.business_impact: If the iteration were replaced with a formula that did not account for each individual day (e.g., a naive subtraction), partial weeks at the start or end of the leave period could be miscounted, leading to incorrect leave balance deductions and potential disputes between employees and HR.

business_rules.title: Cancel All Pending Leaves on Employee Termination
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has one or more leave requests in a pending or approved status, and the employee is being terminated
business_rules.when_clause: The employee termination offboarding workflow triggers leave cancellation for the given employee ID
business_rules.then_clause: All pending or approved future leave requests for that employee must have their status updated to 'cancelled' and persisted to the database
business_rules.business_impact: If this rule is removed, terminated employees would retain active or approved leave requests in the system. This could result in erroneous leave liability calculations, incorrect accrual balances, potential payouts for leave that should have been voided, and data integrity issues in HR reporting. It could also cause confusion for managers or payroll systems that process approved leave, and may violate offboarding compliance requirements.

business_rules.title: Bulk Cancellation Applies to All Qualifying Leaves Without Exception
business_rules.given_clause: An employee being terminated has multiple pending or approved leave requests
business_rules.when_clause: The leave cancellation process iterates over all retrieved pending leaves
business_rules.then_clause: Every single qualifying leave record must be cancelled — no leave is selectively preserved or skipped during termination offboarding
business_rules.business_impact: If partial cancellation were allowed (e.g., only some leaves cancelled), the system would have inconsistent leave records for a terminated employee. This could lead to partial leave payouts, audit failures, and downstream payroll or benefits calculation errors. The policy intent appears to be a clean slate on all outstanding leave obligations at termination.

business_rules.title: Leave Status Transition to 'Cancelled' is the Authoritative Termination State
business_rules.given_clause: A leave request exists in a pending or approved state for an employee undergoing termination
business_rules.when_clause: The cancellation process sets the leave status
business_rules.then_clause: The leave status must be explicitly set to the string value 'cancelled', representing the terminal state for leave records associated with terminated employees
business_rules.business_impact: If the status were set to a different value (e.g., 'rejected', 'withdrawn', or left unchanged), downstream systems relying on the 'cancelled' status to exclude terminated-employee leaves from reporting, accrual calculations, or manager dashboards would malfunction. Consistent use of 'cancelled' as the canonical termination-related leave state is critical for data integrity across integrated HR and payroll systems.

business_rules.title: Final Paycheck Scheduled Upon Employee Termination
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has been terminated and is undergoing the offboarding workflow
business_rules.when_clause: The final paycheck scheduling step is triggered as part of the termination lifecycle
business_rules.then_clause: A final paycheck (including any owed PTO payout) is scheduled for the terminated employee and the event is logged
business_rules.business_impact: If this rule is removed, terminated employees would not receive their final paycheck or owed PTO payout through the automated offboarding workflow, potentially violating labor laws (e.g., state final pay statutes requiring timely payment upon termination), exposing the company to legal liability, wage claims, and penalties.

business_rules.title: PTO Payout Included in Final Paycheck
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is being terminated and may have accrued but unused PTO
business_rules.when_clause: The final paycheck is scheduled as part of the offboarding process
business_rules.then_clause: Any owed PTO payout is included in the final paycheck calculation and scheduling
business_rules.business_impact: If PTO payout is excluded from the final paycheck, employees may not receive compensation for accrued leave they are legally entitled to (depending on jurisdiction), resulting in underpayment, employee disputes, regulatory fines, and reputational damage. Note: the actual PTO payout logic is not visible in this stub, reducing confidence that it is fully implemented here.

business_rules.title: Final Paycheck Is Part of Mandatory Offboarding Workflow
business_rules.given_clause: An employee termination event has been initiated via EmployeeService.terminate_employee
business_rules.when_clause: _trigger_final_paycheck is called as a downstream step in the termination lifecycle
business_rules.then_clause: The final paycheck step is always executed as a non-optional component of the offboarding process, alongside COBRA eligibility, access revocation, and leave cancellation
business_rules.business_impact: If this step is made optional or removed from the offboarding orchestration, terminated employees could be missed in payroll processing, leading to delayed or absent final payments, non-compliance with wage payment laws, and potential class-action exposure if the omission is systemic.

business_rules.title: Active Employment Status Required for Promotion
business_rules.given_clause: An employee exists in the HR system with a non-active employment status (e.g., terminated, on leave, suspended, or inactive)
business_rules.when_clause: A promotion eligibility check is performed for that employee
business_rules.then_clause: The employee is deemed ineligible for promotion and the eligibility check returns false
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would allow terminated, suspended, or otherwise inactive employees to be promoted, resulting in invalid HR records, potential payroll errors, legal compliance issues, and promotion of individuals who no longer have an active employment relationship with the organization. The downstream promote_employee service could execute salary changes and record promotions for non-employees.

business_rules.title: Minimum One Year Tenure Required for Promotion
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is in active employment status but has less than one full year of tenure with the organization
business_rules.when_clause: A promotion eligibility check is performed for that employee
business_rules.then_clause: The employee is deemed ineligible for promotion and the eligibility check returns false
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would allow newly hired employees to be promoted immediately after joining, bypassing the probationary and performance observation period. This undermines talent management policies, could create pay equity issues, devalue promotions as a recognition mechanism, and expose the organization to promoting employees whose performance and cultural fit have not been adequately assessed. The docstring also references 'past probation' as an intent, suggesting this tenure threshold serves as a proxy for probation completion.

business_rules.title: Maximum PTO Carryover Cap of 5 Days
business_rules.given_clause: An active employee has an available PTO balance at year-end
business_rules.when_clause: The year-end carryover cycle is executed
business_rules.then_clause: No more than 5 PTO days (MAX_PTO_CARRYOVER) are carried over to the next year's balance, regardless of how many days the employee has available
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this cap would allow employees to accumulate unlimited PTO across years, creating significant financial liability on the company's balance sheet (accrued PTO is a legal obligation in many jurisdictions) and undermining the 'use it or lose it' policy intent.

business_rules.title: Excess PTO Forfeiture (Use It or Lose It)
business_rules.given_clause: An active employee has more available PTO days than the carryover cap (MAX_PTO_CARRYOVER) at year-end
business_rules.when_clause: The year-end carryover cycle is executed
business_rules.then_clause: All PTO days exceeding the 5-day cap are forfeited and not transferred to the next year's balance
business_rules.business_impact: Removing forfeiture would allow employees to roll over all unused PTO indefinitely, dramatically increasing the company's accrued leave liability, potentially requiring large cash payouts upon termination, and removing the incentive for employees to take time off during the year.

business_rules.title: Sick Leave Excluded from Carryover
business_rules.given_clause: An active employee has sick leave balances at year-end
business_rules.when_clause: The year-end carryover cycle is executed
business_rules.then_clause: Sick leave balances are not processed, carried over, or forfeited — only PTO (LeaveType.PTO) is subject to the carryover cycle
business_rules.business_impact: If sick leave were included in carryover processing, employees could lose sick leave balances they may be legally entitled to retain (many jurisdictions mandate sick leave carryover), exposing the company to legal and compliance risk and reducing employee welfare protections.

business_rules.title: Carryover Calculated from Available (Unused, Non-Pending) Balance Only
business_rules.given_clause: An active employee has a PTO balance that may include pending leave requests
business_rules.when_clause: The year-end carryover cycle is executed
business_rules.then_clause: The carryover amount is derived from 'available_days' (unused, non-pending balance), not the total or gross balance, ensuring pending/approved-but-not-yet-taken leave is excluded from the carryover calculation
business_rules.business_impact: Using gross or total days instead of available days could allow employees to carry over days that are already committed to approved leave requests, resulting in double-counting of leave entitlements and inflated next-year balances.

business_rules.title: Only Active Employees Are Processed for Carryover
business_rules.given_clause: The organisation has both active and terminated/inactive employees
business_rules.when_clause: The year-end carryover cycle is executed
business_rules.then_clause: Only employees returned by find_active_employees() receive a next-year PTO balance; terminated or inactive employees are excluded from carryover processing
business_rules.business_impact: Including terminated employees in carryover would create PTO balance records for people no longer employed, potentially triggering erroneous payouts, inflating HR system data, and creating compliance issues around final pay calculations.

business_rules.title: Employees Without a PTO Balance Record Are Skipped
business_rules.given_clause: An active employee has no PTO balance record for the current year
business_rules.when_clause: The year-end carryover cycle is executed
business_rules.then_clause: The employee is silently skipped and no next-year balance is created for them, preventing errors from missing data
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this guard could cause runtime errors that abort the entire carryover cycle, leaving all subsequent employees unprocessed. However, it could also mask a data integrity issue where active employees are missing expected PTO records, potentially denying them their rightful carryover entitlement.

business_rules.title: Next-Year Balance Initialised with Both Carryover and Fresh Accrual
business_rules.given_clause: An active employee's carryover amount has been calculated
business_rules.when_clause: The year-end carryover cycle is executed
business_rules.then_clause: The employee's next-year PTO balance is created with their tenure-based annual accrual entitlement (total_days) plus the carried-over days tracked separately (carried_over), ensuring both components are recorded distinctly for the new year
business_rules.business_impact: If carryover and accrual were merged into a single field, the organisation would lose auditability of how much of an employee's balance originated from carryover versus fresh entitlement, complicating compliance reporting, termination payout calculations, and leave liability accounting.

business_rules.title: Reuse Existing Leave Balance
business_rules.given_clause: An employee already has a leave balance record for a specific leave type and year
business_rules.when_clause: A request is made to retrieve or create a leave balance for that employee, leave type, and year
business_rules.then_clause: The existing balance is returned without modification and no new balance is created
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, duplicate balance records could be created for the same employee/leave type/year, leading to inflated entitlements, incorrect balance tracking, and potential over-approval of leave requests.

business_rules.title: PTO Entitlement Based on Tenure Accrual
business_rules.given_clause: No existing PTO balance exists for an employee and year, and the employee record can be retrieved
business_rules.when_clause: A new PTO leave balance is being initialised for a known employee
business_rules.then_clause: The total PTO days are calculated using the employee's tenure-based accrual rate (tiered: 15/20/25/30 days per year)
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, all employees would receive the same flat PTO entitlement regardless of tenure, eliminating the reward mechanism for long-serving employees and potentially violating employment contracts or HR policy.

business_rules.title: Default PTO Entitlement for Unknown Employee
business_rules.given_clause: No existing PTO balance exists and the employee record cannot be found in the system
business_rules.when_clause: A new PTO leave balance is being initialised for an unresolvable employee ID
business_rules.then_clause: A default entitlement of 15 PTO days is assigned
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, the system would raise an error or assign zero days when an employee record is missing, potentially blocking leave submissions for valid employees whose records are temporarily unavailable or not yet fully provisioned.

business_rules.title: Fixed Annual Sick Leave Entitlement
business_rules.given_clause: No existing sick leave balance exists for an employee and year
business_rules.when_clause: A new SICK leave balance is being initialised
business_rules.then_clause: The total sick days are set to the system-wide constant SICK_DAYS_PER_YEAR, regardless of employee tenure or role
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or made variable, sick leave entitlements could become inconsistent across employees, creating compliance risks with labour law or company policy that mandates a uniform sick leave allowance.

business_rules.title: Zero Entitlement for Unrecognised Leave Types
business_rules.given_clause: No existing leave balance exists for an employee and year, and the leave type is neither PTO nor SICK
business_rules.when_clause: A new leave balance is being initialised for an unrecognised or other leave type
business_rules.then_clause: The total days entitlement is set to zero
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, unrecognised leave types could inherit an arbitrary or undefined entitlement, potentially granting employees unlimited or erroneous leave days for leave categories not yet formally configured in the system.

business_rules.title: Persist Newly Initialised Leave Balance
business_rules.given_clause: No prior leave balance exists for the employee, leave type, and year combination
business_rules.when_clause: A new leave balance has been calculated and constructed
business_rules.then_clause: The new balance record is saved to the data store so it is available for future retrievals and leave request processing
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, a new balance would be returned in memory but never persisted, causing every subsequent call to re-initialise the balance from scratch, losing any leave taken or reserved and effectively resetting entitlements on every request.

business_rules.title: Entry-Level PTO Accrual: 0–2 Years Tenure
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has a tenure of less than 2 years
business_rules.when_clause: The annual PTO accrual entitlement is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: The employee is entitled to 15 days of PTO per year
business_rules.business_impact: New and early-tenure employees would receive an incorrect PTO entitlement, potentially over- or under-accruing leave. This could lead to compliance issues, employee dissatisfaction, and incorrect payroll/leave liability calculations.

business_rules.title: Junior PTO Accrual: 2–5 Years Tenure
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has a tenure of at least 2 years but less than 5 years
business_rules.when_clause: The annual PTO accrual entitlement is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: The employee is entitled to 20 days of PTO per year
business_rules.business_impact: Employees in the 2–5 year tenure band would not receive the correct accrual uplift that rewards early loyalty. This could undermine retention incentives and create legal/contractual exposure if PTO entitlements are governed by employment agreements.

business_rules.title: Mid-Tenure PTO Accrual: 5–10 Years Tenure
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has a tenure of at least 5 years but less than 10 years
business_rules.when_clause: The annual PTO accrual entitlement is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: The employee is entitled to 25 days of PTO per year
business_rules.business_impact: Mid-career employees would not receive the enhanced leave benefit that recognises sustained service. This could damage morale, increase attrition risk among experienced staff, and create inequitable treatment relative to HR policy commitments.

business_rules.title: Senior PTO Accrual: 10+ Years Tenure
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has a tenure of 10 or more years
business_rules.when_clause: The annual PTO accrual entitlement is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: The employee is entitled to 30 days of PTO per year
business_rules.business_impact: Long-serving employees would be denied the maximum PTO tier, which is a key retention and reward mechanism. This could breach employment contracts, trigger grievances, and undermine the organisation's commitment to rewarding loyalty.

business_rules.title: Tiered Accrual Applies Highest Qualifying Rate
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's tenure qualifies for multiple accrual thresholds
business_rules.when_clause: The annual PTO accrual entitlement is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: The highest applicable accrual rate (i.e. the one corresponding to the highest threshold the employee meets) is applied, not a lower or cumulative rate
business_rules.business_impact: If the highest-qualifying-rate logic were removed or inverted, employees could receive a lower tier than they are entitled to (e.g. a 12-year employee receiving 15 days instead of 30), causing systematic under-accrual, legal liability, and significant employee relations issues.

business_rules.title: Default Accrual Rate for Unmatched Tenure
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's tenure does not match any threshold in the PTO_ACCRUAL_RATES configuration (e.g. the configuration is empty or tenure is 0)
business_rules.when_clause: The annual PTO accrual entitlement is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: The employee defaults to an accrual entitlement of 15 days per year
business_rules.business_impact: Without a safe default, employees with edge-case tenure values or a misconfigured PTO_ACCRUAL_RATES table could receive 0 days of PTO, causing immediate compliance failures, employee complaints, and potential legal action for denial of statutory or contractual leave entitlements.

business_rules.title: FMLA Minimum Employment Duration Requirement
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is requesting FMLA leave and their total tenure is being evaluated
business_rules.when_clause: The employee's total months of employment is less than 12 months
business_rules.then_clause: The FMLA leave request is rejected with an error stating that 12 months of employment are required, along with the employee's current tenure in months
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, employees who have worked for the company for less than 12 months could be granted federally protected FMLA leave. This would violate 29 U.S.C. § 2611 and expose the employer to legal liability for misclassifying leave, potentially creating fraudulent FMLA protections for ineligible employees and distorting workforce planning and leave balance accounting.

business_rules.title: FMLA Minimum Hours Worked Requirement
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has passed the 12-month employment duration check and their hours worked in the past 12 months are being evaluated
business_rules.when_clause: The employee has worked fewer than 1,250 hours in the past 12 months
business_rules.then_clause: The FMLA leave request is rejected with an error stating that 1,250 hours worked in the past 12 months are required, along with the employee's actual hours worked
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, part-time or intermittent employees who have not met the federal hours threshold could be incorrectly granted FMLA-protected leave. This violates 29 C.F.R. § 825.110 and could result in regulatory penalties, wrongful termination claims if such employees are later disciplined, and financial exposure from paying out leave benefits to ineligible workers.

business_rules.title: FMLA Hours Lookup Scoped to Trailing 12-Month Window
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's hours worked are being retrieved to assess FMLA eligibility
business_rules.when_clause: The system queries the database for hours worked by the employee
business_rules.then_clause: Only hours worked within the past 12 months are considered, not the employee's entire employment history
business_rules.business_impact: If the time window were changed (e.g., to lifetime hours or a shorter period), employees who worked heavily in prior years but reduced hours recently could be incorrectly deemed eligible, or employees who recently increased hours could be incorrectly denied. This would misalign with the federal FMLA rolling 12-month measurement period and create compliance violations.

business_rules.title: Approver Identity Required for Payroll Approval
business_rules.given_clause: A request is made to approve a payroll run via POST /payroll/runs/{id}/approve
business_rules.when_clause: The request body does not contain a valid approver_id
business_rules.then_clause: The system rejects the request with HTTP 400 and the message 'approver_id is required', preventing the approval from proceeding
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, payroll runs could be approved anonymously with no audit trail of who authorised payment. This would violate financial controls, make fraud detection impossible, and breach regulatory requirements for authorised payment approval. ACH payments could be initiated without any accountable approver on record.

business_rules.title: Only Authorised Approvers Can Approve Payroll Runs
business_rules.given_clause: A request to approve a payroll run is submitted with a valid approver_id
business_rules.when_clause: The payroll service validates the approver's authority and the approver does not have the required payroll admin role or permission
business_rules.then_clause: The service raises a ValueError which is caught and returned as an HTTP 400 error, blocking the approval and ACH payment initiation
business_rules.business_impact: If this rule were removed, unauthorised personnel could approve payroll runs and trigger ACH payments. This would undermine segregation of duties, expose the organisation to fraudulent or erroneous payroll disbursements, and violate internal financial controls and potentially SOX or equivalent compliance requirements.

business_rules.title: Payroll Approval Triggers ACH Payment Initiation
business_rules.given_clause: A payroll run exists in a processed state and a valid, authorised approver_id is provided
business_rules.when_clause: The payroll approval request is successfully validated and approved
business_rules.then_clause: The payroll run is transitioned to 'approved' status and ACH payment initiation is triggered, with the updated run_id and status returned in the HTTP 200 response
business_rules.business_impact: If this rule were removed or decoupled, approving a payroll run would not automatically initiate ACH payments, meaning employees would not be paid on time. The approval step would become a no-op from a payment perspective, breaking the end-to-end payroll disbursement workflow and potentially causing missed payroll obligations and legal liability.

business_rules.title: Payroll Run Must Be in an Approvable State
business_rules.given_clause: A request to approve a payroll run is submitted with a valid approver_id
business_rules.when_clause: The referenced payroll run does not exist or is not in a state eligible for approval (e.g., already approved, cancelled, or not yet processed)
business_rules.then_clause: The payroll service raises a ValueError which is caught and returned as an HTTP 400 error, preventing invalid state transitions
business_rules.business_impact: Without this guard, duplicate approvals or approvals of invalid/non-existent payroll runs could trigger multiple ACH payment initiations, resulting in duplicate employee payments, financial loss, and complex reconciliation issues. It also prevents approving payroll that has not been fully processed and validated.

business_rules.title: Weekly pay frequency yields 52 periods per year
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is paid on a weekly pay frequency
business_rules.when_clause: The number of pay periods per year is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: The system returns 52 pay periods per year
business_rules.business_impact: If this value is incorrect, weekly employees' gross pay per period would be miscalculated (over- or under-paid), benefit deductions would be incorrectly pro-rated, and annualised salary figures would be wrong, leading to payroll compliance failures and potential legal liability.

business_rules.title: Bi-weekly pay frequency yields 26 periods per year
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is paid on a bi-weekly pay frequency (every two weeks)
business_rules.when_clause: The number of pay periods per year is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: The system returns 26 pay periods per year
business_rules.business_impact: If this value is incorrect, bi-weekly employees would receive incorrect gross pay per period and incorrect deduction amounts. Using 24 instead of 26 (a common error) would result in employees being overpaid by approximately 8.3% per period, causing significant payroll budget overruns.

business_rules.title: Semi-monthly pay frequency yields 24 periods per year
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is paid on a semi-monthly pay frequency (twice per calendar month)
business_rules.when_clause: The number of pay periods per year is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: The system returns 24 pay periods per year
business_rules.business_impact: If this value is incorrect or confused with bi-weekly (26), semi-monthly employees would be over- or under-paid each period. Confusing semi-monthly with bi-weekly is a common payroll error that leads to incorrect tax withholding and benefit deduction amounts.

business_rules.title: Monthly pay frequency yields 12 periods per year
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is paid on a monthly pay frequency
business_rules.when_clause: The number of pay periods per year is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: The system returns 12 pay periods per year
business_rules.business_impact: If this value is incorrect, monthly employees' per-period gross pay and deductions would be miscalculated. Since monthly pay periods are the largest, errors here would result in the largest absolute dollar discrepancies per paycheck.
