business_rules.title: Only supported pay frequencies are permitted in payroll calculations
business_rules.given_clause: A pay frequency value is provided to the payroll calculation engine
business_rules.when_clause: The periods-per-year lookup is performed for a frequency not in the defined set (WEEKLY, BIWEEKLY, SEMIMONTHLY, MONTHLY)
business_rules.then_clause: The system raises a KeyError, rejecting the unsupported frequency and preventing an invalid payroll calculation from proceeding
business_rules.business_impact: If unsupported frequencies were silently allowed (e.g., returning a default value), payroll calculations could proceed with an incorrect divisor, producing systematically wrong pay amounts for all affected employees. The implicit rejection enforces that only officially supported pay schedules are processed.

business_rules.title: Skip Deductions for Unresolvable Benefit Plans
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has an active benefit enrollment referencing a plan ID
business_rules.when_clause: The benefit plan record cannot be found in the system (returns None)
business_rules.then_clause: The enrollment is silently skipped and no deduction is created for that enrollment
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, orphaned or deleted benefit plans would cause runtime errors and prevent payroll processing entirely. If inverted (i.e., still creating deductions for missing plans), employees could receive incorrect or phantom deductions with no valid plan backing them, leading to payroll discrepancies and potential compliance issues.

business_rules.title: Convert Monthly Employee Contribution to Per-Period Deduction Amount
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is enrolled in a benefit plan with a monthly employee contribution amount and a known pay frequency
business_rules.when_clause: Per-period deductions are calculated for a pay run
business_rules.then_clause: The per-period deduction amount is computed as (monthly_contribution × 12) / periods_per_year, rounded to 2 decimal places
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or miscalculated, employees would be over- or under-charged for benefits each pay period. Annual benefit costs would not align with enrollment agreements, causing financial discrepancies, employee complaints, and potential regulatory non-compliance.

business_rules.title: Convert Monthly Employer Contribution to Per-Period Employer Match
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is enrolled in a benefit plan with a monthly employer contribution amount and a known pay frequency
business_rules.when_clause: Per-period deductions are calculated for a pay run
business_rules.then_clause: The per-period employer match is computed as (employer_contribution × 12) / periods_per_year, rounded to 2 decimal places
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, employer benefit contributions would not be tracked per pay period, breaking employer cost accounting, benefits reconciliation, and potentially affecting 401k match reporting and tax filings.

business_rules.title: Map Benefit Plan Type to Canonical Deduction Type
business_rules.given_clause: A benefit plan has a benefit_type value (e.g., 'medical', 'dental', 'vision', 'retirement_401k', 'hsa', 'fsa', 'life_insurance')
business_rules.when_clause: A deduction record is being created for the enrollment
business_rules.then_clause: The deduction is assigned the corresponding canonical DeductionType enum value; unrecognised benefit types default to DeductionType.HEALTH_INSURANCE
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or incorrectly mapped, deductions would be misclassified on pay stubs and in payroll records, causing incorrect tax treatment, inaccurate benefits reporting, employee confusion, and potential IRS/regulatory compliance failures.

business_rules.title: Classify Pre-Tax Benefit Deductions
business_rules.given_clause: A benefit plan has a benefit_type of 'medical', 'dental', 'vision', 'hsa', 'fsa', or 'retirement_401k'
business_rules.when_clause: A deduction record is created for the enrollment
business_rules.then_clause: The deduction is flagged as pre-tax (is_pretax = True); all other benefit types (e.g., 'life_insurance') are flagged as post-tax (is_pretax = False)
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or inverted, taxable income calculations would be incorrect. Pre-tax deductions reduce the employee's taxable gross pay; misclassifying them as post-tax (or vice versa) would result in employees paying incorrect income taxes, incorrect FICA withholding, and potential IRS penalties for the employer. This is a critical tax compliance rule.

business_rules.title: Only Active Enrollments Generate Deductions
business_rules.given_clause: An employee may have multiple benefit enrollments in various states (active, terminated, pending)
business_rules.when_clause: Deductions are calculated for a pay period
business_rules.then_clause: Only enrollments with an active status are retrieved and used to generate deductions
business_rules.business_impact: If removed (i.e., all enrollments regardless of status are used), terminated or pending benefit enrollments would generate deductions, causing employees to be charged for benefits they are no longer enrolled in or not yet entitled to, leading to payroll errors and potential legal liability.

business_rules.title: Zero Overtime Pay for Non-Positive Overtime Hours
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's overtime hours value is zero or negative (i.e., no overtime was worked or an erroneous negative value was supplied)
business_rules.when_clause: Overtime pay is calculated for a pay period
business_rules.then_clause: The overtime pay amount must be exactly $0.00, and no further calculation is performed
business_rules.business_impact: Without this guard, negative overtime hours could produce negative pay adjustments, inflating net pay incorrectly or corrupting pay stubs. Employees could be underpaid or the payroll ledger could carry erroneous negative overtime line items, creating compliance and audit risks.

business_rules.title: Overtime Rate is 1.5x the Derived Hourly Rate (FLSA Compliance)
business_rules.given_clause: A non-exempt employee has worked overtime hours (overtime_hours > 0) and has a known base salary and pay frequency
business_rules.when_clause: Overtime pay is calculated for the pay period
business_rules.then_clause: The overtime pay must equal the number of overtime hours multiplied by the employee's derived hourly rate multiplied by the FLSA-mandated overtime multiplier of 1.5, rounded to two decimal places
business_rules.business_impact: Removing or changing the multiplier would violate the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA), exposing the organisation to regulatory penalties, back-pay liability, and potential class-action lawsuits. Employees would be underpaid for overtime work, creating legal and reputational risk.

business_rules.title: Hourly Rate Derived from Annualised Salary and Standard Weekly Hours
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is salaried (not hourly) and their pay frequency is known
business_rules.when_clause: An hourly rate is needed to compute overtime pay
business_rules.then_clause: The hourly rate must be derived by dividing the per-period salary by the number of standard working hours in that period, where period hours are approximated as STANDARD_WEEKLY_HOURS × (26 / periods_per_year)
business_rules.business_impact: If the hourly rate derivation is incorrect or uses a different hours baseline, overtime pay will be systematically over- or under-calculated for all salaried employees. This creates FLSA compliance exposure and potential payroll inaccuracies affecting every non-exempt salaried worker in the organisation.

business_rules.title: Overtime Pay Rounded to Two Decimal Places (Currency Precision)
business_rules.given_clause: An overtime pay amount has been computed for an employee
business_rules.when_clause: The final overtime pay value is returned
business_rules.then_clause: The result must be rounded to exactly two decimal places to conform to standard monetary representation
business_rules.business_impact: Without rounding, floating-point arithmetic could produce values with many decimal places (e.g., $123.456789), causing discrepancies in payroll totals, tax calculations, and financial reporting. Accumulated rounding errors across many employees could lead to ledger imbalances and failed reconciliation audits.

business_rules.title: Active Employees Are Eligible for Payroll
business_rules.given_clause: A payroll run is being processed and an employee has a status of ACTIVE
business_rules.when_clause: The system determines eligible employees for the payroll run
business_rules.then_clause: The active employee is included in the payroll run
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, actively working employees would not receive paychecks, resulting in wage theft, legal liability, employee dissatisfaction, and regulatory violations.

business_rules.title: Employees on Leave Are Eligible for Payroll
business_rules.given_clause: A payroll run is being processed and an employee has a status of ON_LEAVE
business_rules.when_clause: The system determines eligible employees for the payroll run
business_rules.then_clause: The on-leave employee is included in the payroll run
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, employees on approved leave (e.g., FMLA, parental leave, medical leave) would not receive pay or benefits continuation, violating employment law and company policy, and exposing the organization to legal claims.

business_rules.title: Probationary Employees Are Eligible for Payroll
business_rules.given_clause: A payroll run is being processed and an employee has a status of PROBATION
business_rules.when_clause: The system determines eligible employees for the payroll run
business_rules.then_clause: The probationary employee is included in the payroll run
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, newly hired employees still in their probationary period would not be paid for work performed, constituting wage theft and violating labor laws, and likely causing immediate attrition of new hires.

business_rules.title: Recently Terminated Employees Receive a Final Paycheck
business_rules.given_clause: A payroll run is being processed and an employee has a status of TERMINATED with a termination date on or after the pay period start date
business_rules.when_clause: The system determines eligible employees for the payroll run
business_rules.then_clause: The terminated employee is included in the payroll run to receive their final paycheck
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, employees terminated during the current pay period would not receive their final paycheck, violating final pay laws (e.g., FLSA, state wage payment statutes), exposing the company to penalties, lawsuits, and reputational damage.

business_rules.title: Terminated Employees Outside the Pay Period Are Excluded from Payroll
business_rules.given_clause: A payroll run is being processed and an employee has a status of TERMINATED with a termination date before the pay period start date
business_rules.when_clause: The system determines eligible employees for the payroll run
business_rules.then_clause: The terminated employee is excluded from the payroll run
business_rules.business_impact: If removed (i.e., all terminated employees were included regardless of termination date), former employees who already received their final pay would be paid again, causing overpayments, financial losses, and complex payroll corrections or recovery efforts.

business_rules.title: Terminated Employees Without a Recorded Termination Date Are Excluded from Payroll
business_rules.given_clause: A payroll run is being processed and an employee has a status of TERMINATED but no termination date is recorded
business_rules.when_clause: The system determines eligible employees for the payroll run
business_rules.then_clause: The terminated employee without a termination date is excluded from the payroll run
business_rules.business_impact: If removed (i.e., terminated employees with no termination date were included), employees with incomplete HR records could receive unwarranted payments, creating financial exposure and data integrity issues. Conversely, if the guard is too strict, legitimate final paychecks could be missed due to data entry errors.

business_rules.title: Only Non-Fully-Terminated Employees Are Sourced as Candidates for Payroll
business_rules.given_clause: A payroll run is being processed
business_rules.when_clause: The system fetches the initial pool of employees to evaluate for eligibility
business_rules.then_clause: Only employees who have not been fully removed from the system (i.e., 'active' in the repository sense) are considered as candidates, limiting the evaluation set
business_rules.business_impact: If the repository method were changed to return all employees ever recorded, the eligibility logic would still filter correctly, but performance could degrade significantly. If it were changed to return a narrower set (e.g., excluding recently terminated), eligible terminated employees needing final paychecks could be silently omitted, causing missed final pay obligations.

business_rules.title: Total Tax Withholding Aggregation
business_rules.given_clause: A TaxWithholding record exists with individual tax component values assigned for federal income tax, state income tax, social security, Medicare, additional Medicare, state disability, and local tax
business_rules.when_clause: The total tax withholding amount is calculated for an employee's pay period
business_rules.then_clause: The total withholding must equal the exact arithmetic sum of all seven tax components: federal_income_tax + state_income_tax + social_security + medicare + additional_medicare + state_disability + local_tax
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or miscalculated, employees would have incorrect amounts withheld from their paychecks, leading to under- or over-payment of taxes, potential IRS/state penalties, incorrect W-2 reporting, and legal liability for the employer.

business_rules.title: Federal Income Tax Included in Total Withholding
business_rules.given_clause: A TaxWithholding record exists with a federal_income_tax component
business_rules.when_clause: Total tax withholding is computed
business_rules.then_clause: Federal income tax must be included as a mandatory component of the total withholding amount
business_rules.business_impact: Omitting federal income tax from the total would result in under-withholding of federal taxes, causing employees to owe taxes at year-end and exposing the employer to IRS penalties for failure to withhold.

business_rules.title: State Income Tax Included in Total Withholding
business_rules.given_clause: A TaxWithholding record exists with a state_income_tax component
business_rules.when_clause: Total tax withholding is computed
business_rules.then_clause: State income tax must be included as a mandatory component of the total withholding amount
business_rules.business_impact: Omitting state income tax would cause under-withholding for state obligations, resulting in employee tax liabilities at year-end and potential state tax authority penalties for the employer.

business_rules.title: FICA Social Security Tax Included in Total Withholding
business_rules.given_clause: A TaxWithholding record exists with a social_security component
business_rules.when_clause: Total tax withholding is computed
business_rules.then_clause: Social security (FICA) tax must be included as a mandatory component of the total withholding amount
business_rules.business_impact: Omitting social security tax would violate FICA withholding requirements (26 U.S.C. § 3102), exposing the employer to IRS penalties and creating discrepancies in employee Social Security benefit records.

business_rules.title: Medicare Tax Included in Total Withholding
business_rules.given_clause: A TaxWithholding record exists with a medicare component
business_rules.when_clause: Total tax withholding is computed
business_rules.then_clause: Medicare tax must be included as a mandatory component of the total withholding amount
business_rules.business_impact: Omitting Medicare tax would violate FICA withholding requirements, resulting in IRS penalties and incorrect Medicare contribution records for the employee.

business_rules.title: Additional Medicare Surtax Included in Total Withholding
business_rules.given_clause: A TaxWithholding record exists with an additional_medicare component (applicable to high-income earners above the ACA threshold)
business_rules.when_clause: Total tax withholding is computed
business_rules.then_clause: The additional Medicare surtax (0.9% ACA surtax) must be included as a component of the total withholding amount
business_rules.business_impact: Omitting the additional Medicare surtax for qualifying high-income employees would cause under-withholding, resulting in employee tax liabilities and potential IRS penalties under ACA provisions.

business_rules.title: State Disability Insurance Included in Total Withholding
business_rules.given_clause: A TaxWithholding record exists with a state_disability component
business_rules.when_clause: Total tax withholding is computed
business_rules.then_clause: State disability insurance (SDI) tax must be included as a component of the total withholding amount
business_rules.business_impact: Omitting state disability insurance would result in under-withholding for states that mandate SDI (e.g., California, New York), causing employee shortfalls and employer non-compliance with state labor laws.

business_rules.title: Local Tax Included in Total Withholding
business_rules.given_clause: A TaxWithholding record exists with a local_tax component
business_rules.when_clause: Total tax withholding is computed
business_rules.then_clause: Local tax (e.g., city or county income tax) must be included as a component of the total withholding amount
business_rules.business_impact: Omitting local tax would cause under-withholding for employees subject to municipal or county taxes (e.g., New York City, Philadelphia), resulting in employee tax liabilities and potential local tax authority penalties.

business_rules.title: No Tax Components Excluded from Total
business_rules.given_clause: A TaxWithholding record contains exactly seven recognized tax obligation categories
business_rules.when_clause: Total tax withholding is computed
business_rules.then_clause: All seven components must be summed with no omissions, ensuring the total represents the complete statutory withholding obligation
business_rules.business_impact: If any component is silently excluded, net pay calculations will be overstated, employees will receive more take-home pay than legally permitted, and the employer will face tax authority audits, back-payment demands, and financial penalties.

business_rules.title: Federal Income Tax via Progressive Brackets (Annualized)
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has a known period gross pay and an annual gross pay figure for the current pay period
business_rules.when_clause: Federal income tax withholding is calculated for the pay period
business_rules.then_clause: The annual gross is run through progressive federal tax brackets, and the resulting annual tax is pro-rated back to the period by the ratio (period_gross / annual_gross), rounded to 2 decimal places
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would eliminate federal income tax withholding entirely, causing employees to be under-withheld and exposing the employer to IRS penalties for failure to withhold. Inverting the annualization logic would produce wildly incorrect per-period withholding amounts.

business_rules.title: Zero Federal Tax Guard When Annual Gross Is Zero
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's annual gross pay is zero (e.g., first payroll run or data anomaly)
business_rules.when_clause: Federal income tax withholding is calculated for the pay period
business_rules.then_clause: Federal income tax is set to 0.00 rather than causing a division-by-zero error
business_rules.business_impact: Without this guard, a zero annual gross would cause a runtime division-by-zero exception, halting payroll processing for affected employees. While partly a technical guard, it also encodes the policy that no federal tax is owed when there is no annual income.

business_rules.title: Social Security Tax Capped at Annual Wage Base
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has year-to-date gross earnings on record and a current period gross pay
business_rules.when_clause: Social Security tax withholding is calculated for the pay period
business_rules.then_clause: Only the portion of period gross that keeps cumulative YTD gross at or below the Social Security wage base is subject to the 6.2% Social Security tax; earnings above the wage base are not taxed
business_rules.business_impact: Removing the wage base cap would over-withhold Social Security tax from high earners, violating IRS regulations (IRC §3101), exposing the employer to employee complaints, refund obligations, and potential regulatory penalties. Removing the YTD lookup would restart the cap calculation each period, causing the same over-withholding.

business_rules.title: Social Security Tax Rate of 6.2%
business_rules.given_clause: A taxable Social Security wage amount has been determined for the pay period
business_rules.when_clause: Social Security withholding is computed
business_rules.then_clause: The withholding equals the taxable Social Security wages multiplied by SOCIAL_SECURITY_RATE (6.2%), rounded to 2 decimal places
business_rules.business_impact: Using an incorrect rate would cause systematic under- or over-withholding of Social Security taxes, resulting in IRS penalties, employee tax liabilities, and potential audit findings.

business_rules.title: Medicare Tax Applied to All Period Gross Earnings at 1.45%
business_rules.given_clause: An employee receives gross pay in a pay period
business_rules.when_clause: Medicare tax withholding is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: Medicare tax equals the full period gross pay multiplied by MEDICARE_RATE (1.45%), rounded to 2 decimal places, with no wage base cap
business_rules.business_impact: Removing or capping Medicare tax would under-withhold employee Medicare contributions, violating IRC §3101(b), creating tax liabilities for both employee and employer and triggering IRS penalties.

business_rules.title: Additional Medicare Surtax (0.9%) for High Earners Above Annual Threshold
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's projected annual gross pay exceeds the ADDITIONAL_MEDICARE_THRESHOLD
business_rules.when_clause: Medicare tax withholding is calculated for the pay period
business_rules.then_clause: An additional 0.9% Medicare surtax is withheld on the period gross pay, on top of the standard 1.45% Medicare tax
business_rules.business_impact: Omitting the Additional Medicare Tax would under-withhold for high-earning employees, leaving them with unexpected tax bills at year-end and potentially exposing the employer to IRS under-withholding penalties under the ACA (IRC §3101(b)(2)).

business_rules.title: State Income Tax Applied as Flat Rate by Work State
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is associated with a specific work state that has a defined flat income tax rate
business_rules.when_clause: State income tax withholding is calculated for the pay period
business_rules.then_clause: State income tax equals the period gross pay multiplied by the state-specific flat rate, rounded to 2 decimal places
business_rules.business_impact: Removing state tax withholding would cause employees to owe state taxes at year-end with no withholding credit, and could expose the employer to state tax authority penalties for failure to withhold. Using the wrong state rate would systematically over- or under-withhold for all employees in that state.

business_rules.title: Progressive Federal Income Tax Bracket Application
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has an annual income figure subject to US federal income tax
business_rules.when_clause: Federal tax withholding is calculated for a pay period
business_rules.then_clause: Each portion of income falling within a defined bracket is taxed only at that bracket's marginal rate, and the total tax is the sum of taxes across all applicable brackets
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, income would be taxed at a flat or incorrect rate, causing systematic over- or under-withholding of federal taxes, exposing the employer to IRS penalties and employees to unexpected tax liabilities or refunds.

business_rules.title: Income Below Previous Bracket Limit Is Not Re-Taxed
business_rules.given_clause: The progressive bracket loop is iterating through FEDERAL_TAX_BRACKETS
business_rules.when_clause: The annual income is less than or equal to the lower bound of the current bracket
business_rules.then_clause: Processing stops immediately and no further tax is added for higher brackets
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, the loop would continue into higher brackets with zero or negative taxable amounts, potentially corrupting the tax calculation or causing incorrect zero-tax results for incomes that fall exactly on bracket boundaries.

business_rules.title: Only Income Within the Current Bracket Band Is Taxed at That Rate
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's annual income exceeds the lower bound of a tax bracket
business_rules.when_clause: The taxable amount for a bracket is computed
business_rules.then_clause: Only the income between the bracket's lower bound (prev_limit) and the lesser of the employee's income or the bracket's upper limit is subject to that bracket's rate
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or altered (e.g., applying the top bracket rate to all income), employees would be massively over-taxed, violating IRS regulations and US tax law, and creating significant legal and financial liability for the employer.

business_rules.title: Federal Tax Starts at Zero — No Minimum Tax Floor
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's annual income is zero or below the lowest bracket threshold
business_rules.when_clause: Federal tax withholding is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: The federal income tax owed is zero (no tax is applied)
business_rules.business_impact: If a minimum tax floor were incorrectly introduced, low-income employees would be over-withheld, violating IRS rules and potentially triggering employee complaints, regulatory audits, and refund obligations.

business_rules.title: Federal Tax Brackets Are Applied in Ascending Order
business_rules.given_clause: FEDERAL_TAX_BRACKETS is an ordered sequence of (limit, rate) pairs from lowest to highest income threshold
business_rules.when_clause: The bracket loop iterates over FEDERAL_TAX_BRACKETS
business_rules.then_clause: Each bracket is evaluated sequentially from the lowest income band to the highest, ensuring correct marginal rate assignment at each income tier
business_rules.business_impact: If brackets were applied out of order, the prev_limit tracking would be incorrect, causing wrong taxable band calculations, resulting in either under- or over-withholding and non-compliance with IRS progressive tax rules.

business_rules.title: Default Work State to California When Address Is Absent
business_rules.given_clause: An employee record exists but has no address on file
business_rules.when_clause: The state income tax rate is being determined for payroll processing
business_rules.then_clause: California (CA) is used as the default work state, applying a 7.25% tax rate
business_rules.business_impact: Without this default, employees with missing addresses would either cause a runtime error or receive an incorrect tax rate. Defaulting to CA (one of the highest state tax rates) may over-withhold for employees in lower-tax or no-tax states, creating compliance and employee satisfaction issues. The choice of CA as default may also be legally incorrect if the employee does not work in California.

business_rules.title: Derive Work State From Employee Address
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has a valid address on file
business_rules.when_clause: The state income tax rate is being determined for payroll processing
business_rules.then_clause: The state field from the employee's address is used to look up the applicable tax rate
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, all employees would be taxed at the California default rate regardless of their actual work state, causing systematic over- or under-withholding, potential regulatory penalties, and employee disputes.

business_rules.title: Zero State Income Tax for Texas Employees
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's work state is Texas (TX)
business_rules.when_clause: The state income tax rate is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: A 0.0% state income tax rate is applied, reflecting Texas having no state income tax
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or set to a non-zero value, Texas employees would have state income tax incorrectly withheld, violating Texas law (which has no state income tax), leading to compliance violations and required refunds to employees.

business_rules.title: Zero State Income Tax for Florida Employees
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's work state is Florida (FL)
business_rules.when_clause: The state income tax rate is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: A 0.0% state income tax rate is applied, reflecting Florida having no state income tax
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or set to a non-zero value, Florida employees would have state income tax incorrectly withheld, violating Florida law, leading to compliance violations and required refunds to employees.

business_rules.title: Zero State Income Tax for Washington Employees
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's work state is Washington (WA)
business_rules.when_clause: The state income tax rate is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: A 0.0% state income tax rate is applied, reflecting Washington having no state income tax
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or set to a non-zero value, Washington employees would have state income tax incorrectly withheld, violating Washington law, leading to compliance violations and required refunds to employees.

business_rules.title: Apply California Flat State Income Tax Rate of 7.25%
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's work state is California (CA)
business_rules.when_clause: The state income tax rate is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: A flat 7.25% state income tax rate is applied
business_rules.business_impact: California uses a progressive tax system; this simplified flat rate may under- or over-withhold depending on the employee's income bracket. If changed or removed, California employees would be taxed incorrectly, risking under-withholding penalties or employee over-payment.

business_rules.title: Apply New York Flat State Income Tax Rate of 6.85%
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's work state is New York (NY)
business_rules.when_clause: The state income tax rate is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: A flat 6.85% state income tax rate is applied
business_rules.business_impact: If changed or removed, New York employees would be taxed at an incorrect rate, risking regulatory penalties or employee disputes over incorrect withholding.

business_rules.title: Apply Illinois Flat State Income Tax Rate of 4.95%
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's work state is Illinois (IL)
business_rules.when_clause: The state income tax rate is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: A flat 4.95% state income tax rate is applied
business_rules.business_impact: Illinois has a constitutionally mandated flat income tax; this rate closely reflects actual law. If removed or changed, Illinois employees would be incorrectly withheld, creating compliance risk.

business_rules.title: Apply Pennsylvania Flat State Income Tax Rate of 3.07%
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's work state is Pennsylvania (PA)
business_rules.when_clause: The state income tax rate is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: A flat 3.07% state income tax rate is applied
business_rules.business_impact: Pennsylvania has a flat income tax rate; this value reflects actual law. If removed or changed, Pennsylvania employees would be incorrectly withheld, creating compliance risk.

business_rules.title: Apply Ohio Flat State Income Tax Rate of 4.0%
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's work state is Ohio (OH)
business_rules.when_clause: The state income tax rate is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: A flat 4.0% state income tax rate is applied
business_rules.business_impact: If changed or removed, Ohio employees would be taxed at an incorrect rate, risking regulatory penalties or employee disputes.

business_rules.title: Apply New Jersey Flat State Income Tax Rate of 6.37%
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's work state is New Jersey (NJ)
business_rules.when_clause: The state income tax rate is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: A flat 6.37% state income tax rate is applied
business_rules.business_impact: If changed or removed, New Jersey employees would be taxed at an incorrect rate, risking regulatory penalties or employee disputes.

business_rules.title: Apply Massachusetts Flat State Income Tax Rate of 5.0%
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's work state is Massachusetts (MA)
business_rules.when_clause: The state income tax rate is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: A flat 5.0% state income tax rate is applied
business_rules.business_impact: Massachusetts has a flat income tax; this rate closely reflects actual law. If removed or changed, Massachusetts employees would be incorrectly withheld, creating compliance risk.

business_rules.title: Apply Default 5.0% Tax Rate for Unrecognized States
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's work state is not present in the defined state tax rate table
business_rules.when_clause: The state income tax rate is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: A default flat 5.0% state income tax rate is applied as a fallback
business_rules.business_impact: Without this fallback, employees in unlisted states (e.g., GA, MI, AZ) would either cause an error or receive a 0% rate. The 5.0% default may over- or under-withhold for those states, creating compliance risk and potential employee disputes. Removing it could result in zero withholding for all unlisted states, causing significant under-withholding liability.

business_rules.title: Gross Pay Basis Includes Overtime and Bonus
business_rules.given_clause: A pay stub exists with a base gross pay, overtime pay, and bonus amounts
business_rules.when_clause: Employer payroll taxes are calculated
business_rules.then_clause: The taxable gross used for all employer tax calculations must equal the sum of gross_pay, overtime_pay, and bonus
business_rules.business_impact: If overtime or bonus were excluded, employer taxes would be under-calculated, resulting in tax underpayment, potential IRS penalties, and non-compliance with FICA/FUTA statutory obligations.

business_rules.title: Social Security Employer Tax Capped at Statutory Wage Base
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's combined gross pay (including overtime and bonus) is computed for the pay period
business_rules.when_clause: The employer Social Security (FICA) tax is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: The taxable wages for Social Security must not exceed SOCIAL_SECURITY_WAGE_BASE, and the employer SS tax equals min(gross, SOCIAL_SECURITY_WAGE_BASE) multiplied by EMPLOYER_SS_RATE, rounded to 2 decimal places
business_rules.business_impact: Without the wage base cap, employers would over-remit Social Security taxes on high earners, causing financial loss and incorrect W-2/W-3 filings. Removing the cap entirely violates IRS regulations (IRC §3111).

business_rules.title: Medicare Employer Tax Applied to All Wages Without Cap
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's combined gross pay is computed for the pay period
business_rules.when_clause: The employer Medicare tax is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: Medicare tax must be applied to the full gross pay with no wage base ceiling, equaling gross multiplied by EMPLOYER_MEDICARE_RATE, rounded to 2 decimal places
business_rules.business_impact: If a wage base cap were incorrectly applied to Medicare, high-earning employees would result in under-remittance of Medicare taxes, violating IRC §3111(b) and exposing the employer to IRS penalties and interest.

business_rules.title: FUTA Tax Capped at Statutory FUTA Wage Base
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's combined gross pay is computed for the pay period
business_rules.when_clause: The employer Federal Unemployment Tax (FUTA) is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: The taxable wages for FUTA must not exceed FUTA_WAGE_BASE, and the FUTA tax equals min(gross, FUTA_WAGE_BASE) multiplied by FUTA_RATE, rounded to 2 decimal places
business_rules.business_impact: Without the FUTA wage base cap, employers would over-pay federal unemployment taxes on wages above the statutory threshold, causing financial overpayment and incorrect Form 940 filings. Removing the cap violates IRC §3301.

business_rules.title: Total Employer Tax Is Sum of SS, Medicare, and FUTA
business_rules.given_clause: Employer Social Security, Medicare, and FUTA taxes have each been individually calculated
business_rules.when_clause: The total employer tax liability is returned
business_rules.then_clause: The total must equal the sum of employer_ss, employer_medicare, and employer_futa with no other adjustments or offsets applied
business_rules.business_impact: If any component were omitted from the sum, the employer's total tax liability would be understated, leading to underpayment of payroll taxes, IRS penalties, and inaccurate payroll run totals reported by process_payroll.

business_rules.title: Monetary Tax Amounts Rounded to Two Decimal Places
business_rules.given_clause: A computed employer tax amount results in a floating-point value
business_rules.when_clause: Each individual employer tax component (SS, Medicare, FUTA) is finalized
business_rules.then_clause: Each tax amount must be rounded to exactly 2 decimal places (cents) before aggregation
business_rules.business_impact: Without per-component rounding, floating-point precision errors could accumulate across large payroll runs, causing discrepancies in tax remittance amounts, reconciliation failures, and potential audit issues with tax authorities.

business_rules.title: Pay Date Must Be On Or After Period End Date
business_rules.given_clause: A payroll administrator is creating a new payroll run with a specified pay period start date, pay period end date, and pay date
business_rules.when_clause: The requested pay date is earlier than the pay period end date
business_rules.then_clause: The system rejects the payroll run creation and raises a validation error stating 'Pay date must be on or after the period end date'
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would allow employees to be paid before the work period they are being compensated for has concluded. This creates financial and legal exposure: the company could issue payments for hours or work not yet completed, making payroll corrections and clawbacks necessary. It also violates standard payroll accounting practices and could breach employment contracts or labour regulations that define when wages are earned versus when they are disbursed.

business_rules.title: No Duplicate Payroll Runs For The Same Pay Period
business_rules.given_clause: A payroll administrator is creating a new payroll run and a payroll run already exists in the system for the same pay period start and end dates
business_rules.when_clause: A request is made to create another payroll run covering the identical pay period
business_rules.then_clause: The system rejects the creation and raises a validation error indicating a payroll run already exists for that period, preventing the duplicate from being saved
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would allow multiple payroll runs to be created for the same pay period, resulting in employees potentially being paid multiple times for the same period. This would cause significant financial loss, payroll reconciliation failures, incorrect tax filings, and audit failures. It could also trigger regulatory penalties and erode employee and stakeholder trust in the payroll system's integrity.

business_rules.title: New Payroll Run Is Created In Draft Status
business_rules.given_clause: A payroll administrator has provided valid pay period dates and a valid pay date with no existing run for the same period
business_rules.when_clause: A new payroll run is successfully created
business_rules.then_clause: The payroll run is persisted with a status of 'draft', indicating it has not yet been approved, processed, or finalised
business_rules.business_impact: Removing or bypassing the draft status would collapse the payroll approval workflow, allowing payroll runs to be immediately treated as approved or finalised without human review. This eliminates a critical control gate that prevents erroneous or fraudulent payroll data from being processed and disbursed without authorisation, increasing financial and compliance risk.

business_rules.title: Only Draft Payroll Runs Can Be Processed
business_rules.given_clause: A payroll run exists in the system with a status other than 'draft' (e.g., 'processed', 'cancelled', 'approved')
business_rules.when_clause: A request is made to process the payroll run
business_rules.then_clause: The system raises a ValueError and rejects the processing request, preventing duplicate or unauthorized payroll execution
business_rules.business_impact: Without this guard, payroll runs could be processed multiple times, resulting in duplicate pay stubs, double payments to employees, incorrect tax filings, and financial reconciliation failures. It would also allow processing of cancelled or voided runs, creating fraudulent payment records.

business_rules.title: Payroll Run Must Exist Before Processing
business_rules.given_clause: A run_id is provided that does not correspond to any payroll run in the database
business_rules.when_clause: A request is made to process the payroll run with that run_id
business_rules.then_clause: The system raises a ValueError indicating the payroll run was not found, halting all processing
business_rules.business_impact: Without this check, the system would attempt to process a null object, causing unhandled runtime exceptions. More critically, it prevents processing payroll against non-existent or deleted run records, which could corrupt financial state or allow injection of fraudulent run IDs.

business_rules.title: Only Eligible Employees Are Included in Payroll
business_rules.given_clause: A payroll run in draft status contains a mix of active, on-leave, probationary, terminated, and other-status employees
business_rules.when_clause: The payroll run is processed
business_rules.then_clause: Only employees deemed eligible (active, on-leave, probationary, and recently terminated employees owed a final paycheck) are included; all others are excluded from pay stub generation
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this filter would result in pay stubs and payments being generated for ineligible employees, including fully terminated employees, suspended employees, or pre-hire candidates. This would cause overpayment, legal liability, incorrect tax withholdings, and potential fraud.

business_rules.title: Each Eligible Employee Receives a Calculated Pay Stub
business_rules.given_clause: A set of eligible employees has been determined for a payroll run
business_rules.when_clause: The payroll run is processed
business_rules.then_clause: A complete pay stub (including gross pay, overtime, bonuses, taxes, and deductions) is calculated and appended to the payroll run for every eligible employee
business_rules.business_impact: If pay stubs are not generated per employee, employees would not receive compensation. Skipping stub generation would also break downstream processes such as direct deposit, tax reporting (W-2s, 1099s), and benefits deduction reconciliation.

business_rules.title: Total Gross Pay Aggregates Base Pay, Overtime, and Bonuses
business_rules.given_clause: Pay stubs have been calculated for all eligible employees in a payroll run
business_rules.when_clause: The payroll run totals are aggregated
business_rules.then_clause: The total gross pay is computed as the sum of each employee's gross_pay, overtime_pay, and bonus across all stubs
business_rules.business_impact: If overtime or bonuses are excluded from the gross total, the payroll run's financial summary would understate total labor costs, leading to incorrect general ledger entries, inaccurate budget reporting, and potential misrepresentation in financial statements or audits.

business_rules.title: Employer Payroll Tax Liability Is Calculated and Tracked Separately
business_rules.given_clause: Pay stubs have been calculated for all eligible employees
business_rules.when_clause: The payroll run is processed and totals are aggregated
business_rules.then_clause: The employer's share of payroll taxes (FICA/Social Security, Medicare, FUTA) is calculated per stub and accumulated into a separate total_employer_taxes field on the payroll run
business_rules.business_impact: Without tracking employer taxes separately, the organization would fail to accrue and remit its statutory tax obligations (Social Security match, Medicare match, FUTA). This would result in IRS penalties, interest charges, and potential legal action for tax non-compliance.

business_rules.title: Payroll Run Status Transitions to 'Processed' Upon Completion
business_rules.given_clause: All pay stubs have been calculated and all financial totals have been aggregated for a draft payroll run
business_rules.when_clause: The payroll processing completes successfully
business_rules.then_clause: The payroll run's status is updated to 'processed' and a processed_at timestamp is recorded, marking the run as finalized
business_rules.business_impact: Without the status transition, the payroll run would remain in 'draft' status after processing, allowing it to be processed again (bypassing the draft-only guard on subsequent calls if status is not persisted correctly), causing duplicate payments. The absence of a processed_at timestamp would also break audit trails and compliance reporting.

business_rules.title: Processed Payroll Run Is Persisted to the Database
business_rules.given_clause: A payroll run has been fully processed with all stubs, totals, status, and timestamp updated in memory
business_rules.when_clause: The payroll processing completes
business_rules.then_clause: The updated payroll run record (including all stubs, aggregated totals, 'processed' status, and timestamp) is saved to the persistent data store
business_rules.business_impact: Without persisting the processed run, all calculated pay stubs, financial totals, and the status transition would be lost on process termination. Employees would not receive payment, tax records would not be created, and the run would remain in 'draft' status, allowing reprocessing and creating a data integrity failure.

business_rules.title: Only Processed Payroll Runs Can Be Approved
business_rules.given_clause: A payroll run exists in the system with a status other than 'processed' (e.g., 'draft', 'pending', 'approved', or 'cancelled')
business_rules.when_clause: An approver attempts to approve the payroll run
business_rules.then_clause: The approval is rejected with an error indicating that only processed payroll runs can be approved
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, payroll runs in incomplete, draft, or already-approved states could be re-approved or double-processed, leading to duplicate ACH payments, incorrect disbursements, financial loss, and audit compliance failures. It would break the required sequential payroll lifecycle (draft → processed → approved).

business_rules.title: Only Payroll Admins Can Approve Payroll Runs
business_rules.given_clause: A payroll run is in 'processed' status and an approver is identified
business_rules.when_clause: The approver attempts to approve the payroll run
business_rules.then_clause: The system verifies the approver holds the 'payroll admin' role; if not, the approval is rejected with an authorization error
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would allow any user — including unauthorized employees — to approve payroll runs and trigger ACH payments. This creates a critical segregation-of-duties violation, exposes the organization to fraud, unauthorized fund disbursements, and regulatory non-compliance (e.g., SOX, labor laws). It undermines financial controls entirely.

business_rules.title: Payroll Run Must Exist Before Approval
business_rules.given_clause: An approver provides a payroll run ID for approval
business_rules.when_clause: The system attempts to retrieve the payroll run by the given ID
business_rules.then_clause: If no payroll run is found for the given ID, the approval is rejected with a not-found error
business_rules.business_impact: While partially a technical guard, in a business context this prevents approvals from being recorded against non-existent or deleted payroll runs, which could cause orphaned approval records, ghost ACH payment attempts, and data integrity issues in audit trails.

business_rules.title: Approval Transitions Payroll Run Status to 'Approved' and Records Approver
business_rules.given_clause: A processed payroll run exists and the approver is a payroll admin
business_rules.when_clause: The payroll run is successfully approved
business_rules.then_clause: The payroll run status is updated to 'approved', the approver's identity is recorded on the run, and the updated record is persisted
business_rules.business_impact: Without recording the approver and status transition, the organization loses its audit trail for who authorized payroll disbursements — a critical compliance requirement. It would be impossible to enforce accountability, conduct post-payment audits, or meet regulatory obligations (e.g., SOX, internal controls). Downstream systems relying on 'approved' status to gate further actions would also break.

business_rules.title: Approval Automatically Initiates ACH Payments
business_rules.given_clause: A payroll run has been successfully approved and its status updated to 'approved'
business_rules.when_clause: The approval process completes
business_rules.then_clause: ACH direct deposit payments are automatically initiated for all employees in the payroll run without requiring a separate manual trigger
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would decouple approval from payment initiation, meaning approved payroll runs would never result in employees being paid unless a separate manual step is taken. This would cause missed payroll deadlines, employee dissatisfaction, potential labor law violations for late payment, and break the straight-through processing model the business relies on.

business_rules.title: Overtime Eligibility Restricted to Non-Exempt Employees
business_rules.given_clause: An employee is being processed for a payroll run and their position is identified in the system
business_rules.when_clause: The pay stub calculation is triggered for that employee
business_rules.then_clause: Overtime hours and overtime pay are calculated only if the employee's position is marked as non-exempt; exempt employees receive zero overtime hours and zero overtime pay
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, exempt employees (salaried managers, executives, etc.) would incorrectly receive overtime compensation, violating FLSA regulations, creating significant payroll overpayments, and exposing the company to legal and compliance risk.

business_rules.title: Overtime Pay Calculated at 1.5x Rate for Non-Exempt Employees
business_rules.given_clause: A non-exempt employee has worked overtime hours during the pay period
business_rules.when_clause: Overtime pay is computed during pay stub calculation
business_rules.then_clause: Overtime pay is calculated at 1.5 times the regular hourly rate for all overtime hours, as delegated to _calculate_overtime_pay implementing FLSA compliance
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or the rate changed, the company would violate FLSA overtime requirements, exposing it to back-pay claims, penalties, and lawsuits from affected employees.

business_rules.title: Pending Bonuses Included in Current Period Pay
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has a pending bonus recorded in the system as of the pay period end date
business_rules.when_clause: A pay stub is calculated for the employee
business_rules.then_clause: The pending bonus amount is retrieved and included in the employee's gross compensation for the period; if no bonus exists, the value defaults to zero
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, approved bonuses would not be paid out in the correct pay period, causing employee dissatisfaction, potential contractual breaches, and deferred compensation liability.

business_rules.title: Pending Commissions Included in Current Period Pay
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has a pending commission recorded in the system as of the pay period end date
business_rules.when_clause: A pay stub is calculated for the employee
business_rules.then_clause: The pending commission amount is retrieved and included in the employee's gross compensation for the period; if no commission exists, the value defaults to zero
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, earned commissions would not be disbursed in the correct pay period, violating sales compensation agreements, reducing employee trust, and potentially triggering legal disputes.

business_rules.title: Tax Withholding Applies to Total Taxable Compensation Including Overtime, Bonus, and Commission
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's gross pay, overtime pay, bonus, and commission have been determined for the pay period
business_rules.when_clause: Tax withholding is calculated
business_rules.then_clause: Taxes are computed on the combined total of gross pay, overtime pay, bonus, and commission — not on base gross pay alone
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or scoped only to base pay, supplemental wages (overtime, bonuses, commissions) would be under-withheld, creating tax liabilities for employees and potential IRS penalties for the employer.

business_rules.title: Annual Gross for Tax Bracket Determination Includes Annualized Overtime
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has earned overtime pay in the current pay period
business_rules.when_clause: The annual gross income figure is computed for tax withholding bracket purposes
business_rules.then_clause: The annual gross is calculated as base salary plus overtime pay annualized by multiplying by the number of pay periods per year, ensuring accurate marginal tax rate application
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or simplified to base salary only, employees with significant overtime could be under-withheld on federal and state income taxes, resulting in unexpected tax bills for employees and potential employer liability.

business_rules.title: Benefit Deductions Applied Per Pay Frequency
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has active benefit enrollments with associated contribution amounts
business_rules.when_clause: A pay stub is calculated for the employee
business_rules.then_clause: Benefit deductions are calculated and applied per the pay period frequency, converting monthly contribution amounts to the appropriate per-period amount
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, employees would not have benefit premiums (health, dental, retirement, etc.) withheld from their paychecks, causing benefit plan funding shortfalls and requiring manual correction processes.

business_rules.title: Every Calculated Pay Stub Must Be Persisted
business_rules.given_clause: A complete pay stub has been assembled for an employee with all compensation components and deductions
business_rules.when_clause: The pay stub calculation completes
business_rules.then_clause: The pay stub is saved to the database before being returned, ensuring a durable record exists for audit, reporting, and employee access
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, pay stubs would be calculated in memory but never stored, resulting in loss of payroll records, inability to produce pay history for employees, audit failures, and non-compliance with record-keeping regulations.

business_rules.title: Overtime Calculation Skipped When Position Cannot Be Determined
business_rules.given_clause: An employee's position cannot be found in the system by job title and department
business_rules.when_clause: The pay stub calculation attempts to determine overtime eligibility
business_rules.then_clause: Overtime hours and overtime pay default to zero, preventing erroneous overtime payments when position data is missing
business_rules.business_impact: If the guard were removed and overtime were always calculated regardless of position lookup success, employees with unresolved position data could receive incorrect overtime payments, creating payroll errors and potential overpayments.

business_rules.title: Full-Time Employee Base Pay Calculation
business_rules.given_clause: An employee with full-time employment type and a defined annual base salary
business_rules.when_clause: Gross pay is calculated for a single pay period at a given pay frequency
business_rules.then_clause: The gross pay equals the annual base salary divided by the number of pay periods per year for that frequency
business_rules.business_impact: Without this rule, full-time employees would not receive a correctly annualised per-period salary. Over- or under-payment would occur, leading to payroll compliance failures, employee disputes, and potential legal liability.

business_rules.title: Part-Time Employee 50% Proration Policy
business_rules.given_clause: An employee whose employment type is PART_TIME
business_rules.when_clause: Gross pay is calculated for a single pay period
business_rules.then_clause: The gross pay is reduced to 50% of the equivalent full-time per-period salary, reflecting a fixed part-time proration assumption
business_rules.business_impact: Removing this rule would pay part-time employees at the full-time rate, doubling their intended compensation and causing significant payroll overspend. Inverting it (e.g., applying a different ratio) would misrepresent the agreed employment contract terms and could breach labour agreements.

business_rules.title: Gross Pay Rounded to Two Decimal Places
business_rules.given_clause: A computed gross pay value for any employee type and pay frequency
business_rules.when_clause: The gross pay calculation is finalised before being returned
business_rules.then_clause: The gross pay is rounded to exactly two decimal places (standard monetary precision)
business_rules.business_impact: Without rounding, floating-point precision errors could propagate into pay stubs, tax calculations, and deductions, causing cumulative discrepancies in payroll totals, tax filings, and bank transfer amounts that may violate financial reporting standards.

business_rules.title: Pay Period Frequency Governs Salary De-Annualisation
business_rules.given_clause: An employee with an annual base salary and a specified pay frequency (e.g., weekly, bi-weekly, monthly)
business_rules.when_clause: Gross pay is calculated for a single pay period
business_rules.then_clause: The annual salary is divided by the number of periods per year corresponding to the specified frequency, ensuring the correct per-period amount regardless of how often the employee is paid
business_rules.business_impact: If the frequency mapping were ignored or hardcoded incorrectly, employees paid weekly would receive the same amount as those paid monthly, resulting in either gross overpayment or underpayment depending on the direction of the error, and breaking annualised compensation guarantees.

business_rules.title: ACA Applicable Large Employer Threshold
business_rules.given_clause: An organisation has had its Full-Time Equivalent (FTE) headcount calculated from all employees
business_rules.when_clause: The ACA compliance check is executed
business_rules.then_clause: The organisation is classified as an Applicable Large Employer (ALE) if and only if its FTE count is 50 or more, triggering mandatory coverage obligations
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or the threshold is changed, organisations with 50+ FTEs would either escape ACA obligations (under-enforcement) or organisations below the threshold would be incorrectly burdened with ALE requirements. Either outcome creates significant IRS penalty exposure or unjustified compliance costs.

business_rules.title: ALE-Only Coverage Obligation Enforcement
business_rules.given_clause: The organisation's ALE status has been determined
business_rules.when_clause: The ACA compliance check evaluates employee coverage
business_rules.then_clause: Coverage violation checks are only performed for organisations classified as ALEs; non-ALEs are exempt from the mandatory coverage offer requirement and receive no violations
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, small employers (fewer than 50 FTEs) would be incorrectly flagged for ACA violations they are not legally subject to, generating false penalties and compliance burden. Inverting it would exempt large employers from checks entirely, creating massive IRS Section 4980H penalty exposure.

business_rules.title: Active Employment Status Prerequisite for Coverage Check
business_rules.given_clause: The organisation is an ALE and employee records are being evaluated
business_rules.when_clause: Each employee is assessed for ACA coverage compliance
business_rules.then_clause: Only employees with an ACTIVE employment status are evaluated for coverage violations; inactive, terminated, or otherwise non-active employees are skipped
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, terminated or inactive employees would be incorrectly flagged as coverage violations, producing false compliance reports, wasted HR remediation effort, and potentially incorrect IRS filings.

business_rules.title: Full-Time Employee Definition for ACA Coverage Obligation
business_rules.given_clause: An active employee at an ALE organisation is being evaluated
business_rules.when_clause: The system determines whether the employee triggers a coverage obligation
business_rules.then_clause: Only employees classified as ACA full-time (30+ hours/week or 130+ hours/month) are subject to the mandatory medical coverage offer requirement
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, part-time employees would be incorrectly included in violation checks, overstating compliance obligations. If the threshold were lowered, employers would face obligations beyond what the ACA mandates. Either scenario distorts compliance reporting and could lead to incorrect IRS penalty calculations.

business_rules.title: Mandatory Medical Coverage Offer for ACA Full-Time Employees
business_rules.given_clause: An active, ACA full-time employee works for an ALE organisation
business_rules.when_clause: The system checks whether the employee has been offered medical coverage
business_rules.then_clause: If the employee lacks a medical coverage offer, a violation is recorded citing 'Full-time employee without medical coverage offer' and flagging Section 4980H(a) penalty exposure
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, the core ACA employer mandate would go unenforced within the system. ALEs could unknowingly fail to offer coverage to full-time employees, resulting in IRS Section 4980H(a) penalties which can reach thousands of dollars per employee per year.

business_rules.title: Section 4980H(a) Penalty Risk Classification
business_rules.given_clause: A full-time employee at an ALE has been identified as lacking a medical coverage offer
business_rules.when_clause: A compliance violation record is created for that employee
business_rules.then_clause: The violation is specifically tagged with 'Section 4980H(a) penalty exposure' as the risk classification, distinguishing it from other ACA penalty types
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or mislabelled, compliance officers and legal teams would lose the specific IRS code reference needed to assess financial exposure and prioritise remediation. Section 4980H(a) penalties (employer-level, triggered when no coverage is offered at all) are distinct from 4980H(b) penalties and require different remediation strategies.

business_rules.title: Overall Compliance Status Determination
business_rules.given_clause: All active full-time employees at an ALE have been evaluated for medical coverage
business_rules.when_clause: The compliance report is assembled
business_rules.then_clause: The organisation is marked as compliant only if zero violations were found; the presence of any single violation renders the organisation non-compliant
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or changed to a threshold-based rule (e.g., compliant if fewer than 5% violations), organisations with known coverage gaps could be incorrectly reported as compliant, masking IRS penalty exposure and undermining the integrity of the compliance programme.

business_rules.title: Compliance Violation Warning Logging
business_rules.given_clause: The ACA compliance check has completed and violations have been identified
business_rules.when_clause: The compliance result is finalised
business_rules.then_clause: A warning-level log entry is emitted recording the number of violations found, enabling audit trails and operational alerting
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, compliance violations would produce no operational alert, delaying detection and remediation. Audit trails required for regulatory defence would be incomplete, potentially worsening IRS penalty outcomes.

business_rules.title: Clean Compliance Informational Logging
business_rules.given_clause: The ACA compliance check has completed and no violations were found
business_rules.when_clause: The compliance result is finalised
business_rules.then_clause: An info-level log entry is emitted confirming the check passed along with the FTE count, providing an auditable record of clean compliance
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, there would be no positive audit record of compliance checks that passed, making it harder to demonstrate due diligence to regulators or auditors and potentially weakening the organisation's defence in an IRS inquiry.

business_rules.title: Only Active or On-Leave Employees Included in FTE Count
business_rules.given_clause: A list of employees is provided for ACA FTE calculation
business_rules.when_clause: The FTE count is being calculated for ACA Applicable Large Employer determination
business_rules.then_clause: Only employees with an employment status of ACTIVE or ON_LEAVE are counted; terminated, suspended, or otherwise inactive employees are excluded from the FTE calculation
business_rules.business_impact: If removed, terminated or inactive employees would be incorrectly included in the FTE headcount, potentially inflating the count above the 50-FTE ACA threshold and falsely triggering Applicable Large Employer status, exposing the organisation to unwarranted compliance obligations or, conversely, if inverted, causing genuine large employers to evade ACA mandates and face IRS penalties.

business_rules.title: Full-Time Employees Count as 1.0 FTE Each
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has an employment status of ACTIVE or ON_LEAVE and an employment type of FULL_TIME
business_rules.when_clause: The FTE count is being calculated for ACA Applicable Large Employer determination
business_rules.then_clause: Each full-time employee contributes exactly 1.0 to the total FTE count, regardless of their actual hours worked
business_rules.business_impact: If removed or altered (e.g., using actual hours for full-time employees), the FTE calculation would deviate from IRS ACA methodology, producing incorrect ALE determinations. Undercount could cause large employers to miss ACA obligations; overcount could impose undue compliance burdens on smaller employers.

business_rules.title: Part-Time Employee Hours Are Aggregated Before FTE Conversion
business_rules.given_clause: An employee has an employment status of ACTIVE or ON_LEAVE and an employment type of PART_TIME
business_rules.when_clause: The FTE count is being calculated for ACA Applicable Large Employer determination
business_rules.then_clause: The average monthly hours of each part-time employee are retrieved and summed into a single aggregate part-time hours total, rather than converting each employee individually
business_rules.business_impact: If part-time employees were converted to FTE individually (each divided by 120 and rounded down separately), the result would almost always be lower than the correct aggregate method, systematically under-reporting FTE count and potentially allowing employers above the 50-FTE threshold to incorrectly avoid ALE classification and associated ACA penalties.

business_rules.title: Part-Time FTE Conversion Uses IRS 120-Hour Monthly Divisor
business_rules.given_clause: The total aggregated monthly hours for all active/on-leave part-time employees have been calculated
business_rules.when_clause: The FTE count is being calculated for ACA Applicable Large Employer determination
business_rules.then_clause: The aggregate part-time hours are divided by 120 (the IRS-prescribed monthly hour divisor) and truncated to an integer to produce the part-time FTE equivalent
business_rules.business_impact: If the divisor were changed (e.g., to 130, which is the full-time threshold, or any other value), the part-time FTE conversion would be non-compliant with IRS ACA regulations. A higher divisor would under-count FTEs, potentially allowing large employers to evade ALE status; a lower divisor would over-count, imposing ACA obligations on employers below the legal threshold.

business_rules.title: Part-Time FTE Fractional Remainder Is Discarded (Floor Truncation)
business_rules.given_clause: The aggregate part-time hours have been divided by 120
business_rules.when_clause: The FTE count is being calculated for ACA Applicable Large Employer determination
business_rules.then_clause: Any fractional FTE resulting from the division is truncated (floored) to the nearest whole number using integer conversion, consistent with IRS ACA guidance that disallows fractional FTE carry-over
business_rules.business_impact: If rounding were used instead of truncation, the FTE count could be inflated by up to 0.99 FTE, potentially pushing an employer just below the 50-FTE threshold over it and incorrectly triggering ACA Applicable Large Employer obligations and associated penalties.

business_rules.title: Total FTE Is the Sum of Full-Time Count and Part-Time FTE Equivalent
business_rules.given_clause: The full-time employee count and the part-time FTE equivalent have both been calculated
business_rules.when_clause: The FTE count is being finalised for ACA Applicable Large Employer determination
business_rules.then_clause: The total FTE count returned is the arithmetic sum of the full-time employee count and the integer part-time FTE equivalent, as required by IRS ACA methodology
business_rules.business_impact: If either component were omitted from the sum (e.g., only full-time or only part-time FTEs returned), the ALE determination would be materially incorrect, either causing compliant employers to be flagged as non-compliant or allowing genuinely large employers to evade ACA coverage mandates and face IRS employer shared responsibility penalties.
